The topic of self-driving cars is highly debated especially in light of recent event with the death of an Arizona woman at the hands of a self-driving Uber. Those for self-driving cars argue that they reduce the rate of accidents as a result of distracted drivers, improve traffic flow, are cost saving in terms of time and monetary value from insurance, and could allow greater access to transportation for the disabled and elderly. Those against self-driving cars argue the system does not eliminate accidents, that there is a learning curve with the technology, that it would take away profit from other sectors of the economy, and that there are instances in which the technology is not equipped to make a decision. While both sides make valid points, the benefits of adopting a system of self-driving cars rises to the forefront. 

Many people are quick to point out the flaws in self-driving cars but it is still in its infancy and has the ability to develop and improve with time whereas we have not seen that in human motorists over the years. Certainly we may expect the number of accidents by self-driving cars to rise as it is more widely adopted but this number palls in comparison to the number of accidents caused by drivers. Tragedies such as those in Arizona should not happen but unfortunately with nearly 1.3 million people dying in motor vehicle accidents a year and 81 percent of accidents being the result of human error it appears the main culprit is us. As humans we already rely on computers and technology for many daily tasks and self-driving cars are an extension of the innovative mindset our tech fields have embraced. 

Those against also argue that investing in self-driving cars may take away money from other industries and sectors of the economy. Again while self-driving cars may impact the profits of other transportation system, overall the profits from developing and implementing this technology will outweigh the losses. There is a reason many people invest in tech companies as the have become more profitable in the 21st century. Especially with the start-up of new technology, as they industry grows this will demand that more and more jobs be created to replace the ones lost. Also, savings in terms of reduced accidents are astronomical. The U.S. Department of Transportation has assigned a value of 9.2 million for each human life, with savings coming from venues such as healthcare and insurance. There is also the argument that self-driving cars may also save you time as they free up your transportation time to attend to other daily tasks when you do not have to focus on the driving. With more efficient driving the self-driving cars have the potential to decrease driving time and traffic as well.

Though people are still resistant to the idea of self-driving cars it is already in ways being implemented into our driving with features such as autonomous braking, self-parking and sensor detectors. The technology for this field is currently available and calls on our investment to develop and improve. While no system is perfect, one that saves us lives and on costs should be highly considered.

output_text:
The topic of self-driving cars is highly debated, especially in light of the recent death of an Arizona woman involving a self-driving Uber. Proponents argue that autonomous vehicles reduce accidents caused by distracted drivers, improve traffic flow, save time and money through lower insurance and healthcare costs, and expand transportation access for the elderly and disabled. Opponents contend that the technology does not eliminate accidents, involves a learning curve, could reduce profits in other sectors, and sometimes cannot make the right ethical decision. While both sides make valid points, the benefits of adopting self-driving cars outweigh the drawbacks.

Many people are quick to point out the flaws in self-driving cars, but the technology is still in its infancy and can develop and improve over time in ways human motorists have not. Certainly, we may expect the number of accidents involving self-driving cars to rise as adoption increases, but this number pales in comparison to accidents caused by human drivers. Tragedies such as the Arizona incident should not happen, but with nearly 1.3 million people dying in motor vehicle accidents each year and 81 percent of accidents resulting from human error, the main culprit appears to be us. As humans, we already rely on computers and technology for many daily tasks, and self-driving cars are a natural extension of the innovative mindset embraced in the tech field.

Opponents also argue that investing in self-driving cars may divert money from other industries and sectors of the economy. While autonomous vehicles may affect profits in other transportation systems, the overall returns from developing and implementing this technology will likely outweigh the losses. There is a reason many people invest in tech companies: they have become more profitable in the 21st century. As new technologies emerge, industry growth will demand that more jobs be created to replace those lost. Savings from reduced accidents are substantial. The U.S. Department of Transportation has assigned a value of $9.2 million to each human life, producing savings in areas such as healthcare and insurance. Self-driving cars may also save time by freeing passengers to attend to other tasks while traveling. With more efficient driving, autonomous vehicles have the potential to decrease travel time and reduce traffic congestion.

Although some remain resistant to self-driving cars, the technology is already being implemented in features such as autonomous braking, self-parking, and sensor detectors. The technology exists and calls for continued investment to develop and improve. While no system is perfect, one that saves lives and reduces costs should be seriously considered.

draft1:
Self-driving car is a new technology and receives a lot of debates recently. On one hand, self-driving cars offer positive impacts, e.g. safer commuting, cost savings, and give opportunities to many people who can't travel by themselves at this moment. On the other hand, people also raise concerns about the security, safety, and economy of the technology.
In my opinion, I support bringing self-driving cars into reality. The technology can bring many benefits to humanity. First, we, as human in modern societies, will save a lot of money thanks to the technology. Because when a lot of self-driving cars are on the street and they can communicate with each other, they can reduce the problem with congestion or unknown traffic conditions. Second, self-driving cars are supposed to be more safety because it can eliminate mistakes made by a human. The most common mistake is driving under the influence (of alcohol, drugs, or tireness). Moreover, drivers tend to have false confidence as they have more driving hours. This false confidence will increase the chances that an accident will happen. Last but not least, self-driving cars put an equity for people having difficulties in traveling, such as old people or disabilities. While public transportations are available in all cities, in many cases, they can only take a person to a pre-designated location (stop) rather than the real destination. Public transportations are operated with a fixed schedule which does not perfectly match everyone's situations. 
The cons about self-driving cars could be categorized into two groups: not-ready and not-true. The not-ready group contains arguments about new challenges that the technology will bring in when it is deployed in mass. For example, when an accident is made by a self-driving car, who will hold the responsibility? Or security issues such as how can drivers protect their personal data and what will happen if the car is hijacked by a hi-tech criminal? Interestingly, what would the car-makers or software designers choose in moral-sensitive situations? For example, given an unavoidable accident will happen, which will either kill an old pedestrian crossing the street or a baby in a stroller on the pavement, which choice should be made by the self-driving car? These issues must be addressed or disclosed before deploying self-driving cars in the mass. The not-true group consists of false assumptions about the self-driving technology. For example, there are concerns about drivers are no longer equipped with the skills to operate cars. In fact, we are living well in a society without knowing much about our daily stuffs. I don't know anything about fixing a car, but I can always call AAA or my friends whenever my car breaking down. I use the washing machine at least twice a week without knowing how to make such a machine. Another false opinion is the difference between states regarding turning right on red or cars cannot interpret police officers' signals when directing traffics. In fact, the car can update its algorithm according to the state it is traveling simply based on GPS signal. Moreover, when all cars on the streets are self-driving cars, the police officer does not need to go down the street and directs the traffic but just sends information to the car through a communication channel, e.g. 3G or wifi. Last but not least, some people even afraid that auto manufacturers might sell fewer cars. This is not true as a self-driving car is still a car which needs a manufacturer. In fact, we have seen more and more car manufacturers join the self-driving race nowadays, such as Volvo, BMW, Toyota, Honda, and many more.
In conclusion, I think self-driving car is a revolution that will have more benefit to our society. However, there are still some concerns needed to be addressed in order to make this technology available to everyone.

output_text:
Self-driving cars are a new technology that has generated considerable debate recently. On one hand, they promise positive impacts such as safer commuting, cost savings, and new opportunities for people who currently cannot travel independently. On the other hand, people raise valid concerns about security, safety, and economic effects.

I support bringing self-driving cars into reality because the technology can bring many benefits to society. First, modern societies will save a lot of money as self-driving cars become common. When many autonomous vehicles share the road and communicate with one another, they can reduce congestion and respond more efficiently to traffic conditions. Second, self-driving cars should be safer because they can eliminate many human errors. The most common mistakes include driving under the influence of alcohol or drugs and driving while tired. Drivers also develop false confidence after many hours behind the wheel, which increases the likelihood of accidents; autonomous systems would not suffer from these problems. Last, self-driving cars would increase equity for people who have difficulty traveling, such as older adults or people with disabilities. Public transportation is available in many cities, but it often takes passengers only to predesignated stops rather than to their exact destinations. Public transit also runs on fixed schedules that do not perfectly match everyone’s needs. Door-to-door autonomous vehicles could fill those gaps.

The drawbacks of self-driving cars fall into two groups: “not ready” and “not true.” The “not ready” group contains arguments about new challenges the technology will bring when deployed at scale. For example, when an accident involves a self-driving car, who will be held responsible? What about security issues: how can users protect personal data, and what happens if a car is hijacked by a sophisticated criminal? A related concern is how carmakers or software designers will program cars to respond in morally sensitive situations. If an unavoidable crash will either kill an older pedestrian crossing the street or a baby in a stroller, what choice should the autonomous system make? These issues must be addressed and disclosed before self-driving cars are deployed widely.

The “not true” group consists of false assumptions about the technology. One worry is that human drivers will lose the skills to operate vehicles. Yet we already live in a society where people rely on specialists and services for many everyday tasks: I do not know how to fix my car, but I can call roadside assistance or a friend when it breaks down. I use a washing machine at least twice a week without knowing how it works. Another misconception concerns differences in traffic rules between states—for example, turning right on red or interpreting police officers’ signals. A car can update its software to follow the rules of the state it is in using GPS data, and when streets are predominantly occupied by self-driving vehicles, police officers could send instructions to cars directly via secure communication channels (e.g., cellular networks or Wi‑Fi). Finally, some fear that automakers will sell fewer cars. This is unlikely: a self-driving vehicle is still a car and still requires manufacturers. In fact, more manufacturers—such as Volvo, BMW, Toyota, and Honda—are joining the self-driving race.

In conclusion, I believe self-driving cars represent a revolution that will benefit society overall. However, important concerns about liability, security, and ethical decision-making must be addressed to make this technology safe and accessible for everyone.

Self-driving cars, though only recently becoming a technological feasibility, have advanced rapidly in their short time of development and have thoroughly captured the public eye. The debate on their fitness to our automotive environment and on their desirableness to our society as a whole is raging onward with impassioned argument on both sides. In this essay I will aim to provide convincing evidence that though it is not perfect, the prospect of driver-less roads would mark a definitive improvement over our current conditions. 

To start with, consider the fallibility of human drivers. On a regular basis we turn on our engines without sufficient sleep, absentmindedly forget to check blind-spots, and allow our attention to be torn from the road by the slightest of mobile vibrations. With an estimated 33,000 annual fatal accidents and overwhelming evidence that human error accounts for a significant number of these it seems incontrovertible that we should do better. Although it is not yet certain that self-driving cars in their current condition would cause that number to go down, the lack of human fallibility makes the prospect seem likely. Furthermore, whereas human driver tend to fall into a lull of false confidence when they have engaged in a task for an extended period of time, self-driving cars can be designed to improve and become more safe over time. There is no distraction, no sleep deprivation, no intoxication, and no frantic concern for being late to take a self-driving car away from the task of safely getting from point A to point B. 

Furthermore, self-driving cars could give society a method of transportation which is much less restrictive. Currently, driving privileges are limited to those capable of operating the machinery, passing their test, and -for the most part - affording to own their own vehicle. If a child needs to be transported to any sort of extracurricular event they are bound by their parents' schedules and mobilities. If a senior citizen who has been deemed unsafe to drive decides to go shopping they must wait for long periods to use public transportation with conditions that could be extremely painful or uncomfortable with those who experience chronic pain from arthritis or similar conditions. Furthermore, many who cannot afford their own car are forced to commute long distances to their jobs, health-care, and programs, forcing a de facto restriction on who may access what. Although it is likely that one who cannot afford a regular car could not afford their own self-driving car, there is nothing to say that they must have their own. The ability to have a car drive back to the place it left from could allow a much more intricate form of car sharing; additionally, the current restrictions on insured drivers that hassle any type of shared vehicle would likely be thoroughly relaxed or missing entirely for a driver-less car. Even beyond the prospect of shared cars, self-driving vehicles could revolutionize public transportation, making a wider range of pick up locations and drop off locations feasible. If large buses or a huge fleet of public cars were to have flexible routes that could give people who do not have the means to own their own car a transportational freedom akin to it at a fraction of the price that services like Uber and Lift offer today. 

A society which supports self-driving cars would certainly look different from ours. How we implement them will determine much of their success and failure. For that reason it is not a guaranteed success, however, looking at the massive limitations and problems with our current system, it does not seem to be a stretch to claim that this could bring us dramatic improvements.

Self-driving cars, though only recently having become a technological feasibility, have advanced rapidly in their short time of development, managing to thoroughly capture the public eye. A debate on their fitness to our automotive environment and on their desirableness to our society as a whole has been raging onward with impassioned arguments on both sides. In this essay I aim to provide convincing evidence that, though they have potential to cause societal harm, given the distressing array of negative societal impacts associated with human-driven cars, the apparent feasibility for self-driving cars to ameliorate these issues makes it only rational to welcome their implementation. 
	To start with, consider the fallibility of human drivers. On a regular basis we turn on our engines without sufficient sleep, absentmindedly forget to check blind-spots, and allow our attention to be torn from the road by the slightest of mobile vibrations. With an estimated 33,000 annual fatal accidents and overwhelming evidence that human error accounts for a significant number of these, it seems incontrovertible that we should do better. 
	While no one would rightly argue that our current system is ideal, skepticism toward self-driving cars at this stage is more than fair. The development of the vehicles is very much still in its developmental infancy. Coupling that fact with the reality of a mixed automotive environment - with both computerized and human driven cars - one might very well expect implementation of self-driving cars to cause an increase in accidents. The key question then becomes: "would a self-driving car actually be any safer than a human-driven one?". While there is currently insufficient information to give a definite answer, I think there are good reasons to put more faith in the self-driving cars. Whereas human drivers tend to fall into a lull of false confidence after driving for many years, self-driving cars can be designed to improve and become more safe over time. Further, there is no distraction, no sleep deprivation, no intoxication, and no frantic concern for being late to take a self-driving car away from the task of safely getting from point A to point B. In the absence of such risks, a computerized car might very well behave safer even without the ability to intuitively grasp tendencies and perceptions of neighboring human drivers. 
	Others however, are more concerned about the societal impact of these cars than their safety specs. Even if the change means less lives lost on the road, it still may be an unwise one if it leads to the ruin of lives off the road. Given the number of transportation jobs, a computerized revolution could displace the occupations of many currently employed drivers. Furthermore, given the massive economic investment needed to own such a car, might this lead to a widening of already egregious inequality gaps in the country? 
	The transportation industry represents a real threat if we have no place for the newly unemployed to turn. This however, is not a problem unique to self-driving cars but rather, is a pervasive and imminent threat encroaching on us from all directions given the rapid advance of technological competency. If we cannot, as a society solve this more generally, the threats will be realized even in the absence of the driver-less car revolution; if we do find a way to support the displaced workers of other sectors I remain hopeful that such approaches can be implemented to the transportation-sector as well. 
	As for societal inequality, self-driving vehicles, if implemented properly, could actually be a great boon to economically struggling classes. To begin, the previously considered transportation industry automation, though bad for workers, could be fantastic for commodity prices. If the cost of all daily goods dropped, many would be the better for it. Further, current systems of public transportation are woefully insufficient to serve their desired societal role. When jobs, hospitals, and enrichment events are inconvenient to access, attendance from under serviced individuals tends to drop. This creates a de-facto restriction on who may access what. With an automated public transportation system, it could be much easier to introduce flexible pick-up locations, drop-off spots, and schedules. With a more functional and convenient public transportation system, we could relieve both the economic burden of buying a vehicle as well as the logistical burden of navigating tricky and time-consuming bus routes. 
	A society which supports self-driving cars would certainly look different from ours. How we implement them will determine whether those differences were successes or failures. For that reason it is not a guaranteed success, however, looking at the massive limitations and problems with our current system, it is reasonable to maintain optimism about the ability of self-driving cars to bring about dramatic improvements to our lives.

output_text:
Self-driving cars, though only recently becoming technologically feasible, have advanced rapidly and have thoroughly captured the public eye. The debate over their fitness for our roads and their desirability for society is heated, with impassioned arguments on both sides. In this essay I argue that, while not perfect, the prospect of driverless roads would represent a clear improvement over our current conditions. I acknowledge that self-driving technology raises legitimate concerns—about safety in its early stages, ethical decision-making, and job displacement—but I maintain that these challenges can be managed and do not outweigh the potential benefits.

To start, consider the fallibility of human drivers. On a regular basis we turn on our engines without sufficient sleep, absentmindedly forget to check blind spots, and allow our attention to be torn from the road by the slightest mobile vibration. With an estimated 33,000 annual fatal accidents and overwhelming evidence that human error accounts for a significant number of these, it seems incontrovertible that we should do better. Although it is not yet certain that current self-driving systems would immediately reduce that number, the absence of human frailties makes such an improvement likely. Whereas human drivers tend to fall into a lull of false confidence after extended periods at the wheel, self-driving cars can be designed to improve and become safer over time. There is no distraction, no sleep deprivation, no intoxication, and no frantic concern about being late to divert a self-driving vehicle from the task of getting passengers safely from point A to point B.

Moreover, self-driving cars could provide a much less restrictive method of transportation. Currently, driving privileges are limited to those capable of operating the machinery, passing a test, and, for the most part, affording to own a vehicle. If a child needs to be transported to an extracurricular event, they are bound by their parents’ schedules and mobility. If a senior citizen deemed unsafe to drive wants to go shopping, they may have to wait long periods for public transportation, which can be painful or uncomfortable for those with chronic conditions like arthritis. Many who cannot afford their own car are forced to commute long distances to jobs, health care, and programs, creating a de facto restriction on access. Although someone who cannot afford a regular car may not be able to afford their own self-driving vehicle, they would not necessarily need one. A car that can drive itself back to its origin enables more intricate forms of car sharing; additionally, current insurance and regulatory restrictions that complicate vehicle sharing might be relaxed or redesigned for driverless cars. Beyond shared private vehicles, self-driving fleets could revolutionize public transportation by offering a wider range of pick-up and drop-off locations. Large buses or fleets of small autonomous vehicles with flexible routes could provide a level of transportation freedom comparable to car ownership at a fraction of the cost of services like Uber and Lyft.

A society that embraces self-driving cars would certainly look different from ours, and implementation will determine much of their success or failure. For that reason, adoption is not guaranteed to be an unqualified success. However, given the massive limitations and problems with our current system—chiefly human error, unequal access, and inefficiencies—it is reasonable to claim that autonomous vehicles could bring dramatic improvements.

There are many advantages for the United States to transition to self-driving cars. The primary benefit is simply the amount of lives saved due to the elimination of intoxicated, distracted, or otherwise dangerous drivers. Computers provide a precise, safe alternative to the potential for human error that occurs each time a driver gets behind the wheel. In addition, self-driving cars can save money on insurance costs and outdated infrastructure, decrease traffic, and are actually better for the environment.  However, some limitations to self-driving cars exist. These include a difficult transition to using these vehicles, cost, the failure of technology, and the potential for job loss and decline of certain industries.
	Frequently, we hear arguments for or against something with the justification that it benefits or does not benefit society.  In order to make an argument for either one of these, it is imperative that we consider the following questions: who is society, what is good for society, what does society need, and at what expense to the individual do we support the public good?
	In this case, the answer to these questions is easy. Anything that has the potential to save human lives is both good for the individual and good for society. Despite the limitations in technology, an initial cost, and a likely challenging transition, self-driving cars are the future, and they will save lives. No other benefit matters. No other limitation should stop us.  
America has a serious, dangerous driving problem. Every year, 33,000 people die in automobile accidents, and most of these deaths should have been prevented because the drivers were distracted, drunk, tired, or simply should not have been driving.  
If that statistic is not sobering enough, consider this. Vehicular accidents are also the number one cause of death for teenagers, again, the majority of which are preventable.  We know from cognitive scientists and psychologists that teenagers are more likely to engage in risky behavior—like dangerous, drunk, or otherwise impaired driving—and, while getting a driver’s license has been a rite-of-passage for a century in America, it’s time to stop the senseless destruction car accidents cause.
Interestingly enough, the Department of Transportation calculated that each person is worth $9.2 million. Like so many things, the government got this one wrong as well. A human life is priceless. Nothing can bring back a loved one who has been killed.
If you have reservations about self-driving cars due of the potential for technological failure, I share them with you. Just this past month, a self-driving car being tested in Arizona turned a corner, struck, and killed a woman who was crossing the street outside of a crosswalk. We have not perfected this technology. We have miles to go before it can be implemented on any kind of large scale. We have many ethical, legal, and policy considerations and implications to work through. However, we have the opportunity to save countless lives. We have the opportunity to save countless others from debilitating injury. output_text:
There are many advantages for the United States in transitioning to self-driving cars. The primary benefit is lives saved by eliminating intoxicated, distracted, or otherwise dangerous drivers. Computers provide a precise, safer alternative to the human error that occurs every time someone gets behind the wheel. In addition, self-driving cars can reduce insurance costs, decrease the need for outdated infrastructure, alleviate traffic congestion, and be better for the environment. However, some limitations to self-driving cars exist: a difficult transition to using these vehicles, high initial costs, technological failures, and potential job losses in certain industries.

When evaluating policies that affect the public, we should ask: who is society, what is good for society, what does society need, and at what expense to the individual do we support the public good? In this case, the answers point clearly in favor of widespread adoption of self-driving cars. Anything that has the potential to save human lives benefits both individuals and society. Despite technological limitations, initial costs, and a challenging transition, self-driving cars are the future, and they will save lives. No other benefit matters as much; no other limitation should prevent us from pursuing this technology responsibly.

America has a serious, dangerous driving problem. Every year, about 33,000 people die in automobile accidents, and many of these deaths could have been prevented because drivers were distracted, drunk, fatigued, or otherwise unfit to drive. Vehicular accidents are also the leading cause of death for teenagers, the majority of which are preventable. Cognitive scientists and psychologists show that teenagers are more likely to engage in risky behavior—such as dangerous or impaired driving—and while getting a driver’s license has been a rite of passage for a century in America, we must stop the senseless destruction caused by car accidents.

Some may point to cost or technological failure as reasons to delay adoption. If you have reservations about self-driving cars because of potential technological failure, I share them. Just last month, a self-driving car being tested in Arizona struck and killed a woman who was crossing the street outside a crosswalk. We have not perfected this technology; we have many miles to go before it can be implemented at scale. There are ethical, legal, and policy considerations to work through.

Yet these concerns do not outweigh the potential to save countless lives and prevent debilitating injuries. The proper response to technological risk is not rejection but regulation, rigorous testing, and phased deployment. Implementing strict safety standards, improving sensor and software reliability, and creating liability frameworks can reduce the risk of fatal incidents. Retraining programs and economic support can mitigate job losses in affected industries. In short, while self-driving cars are not a panacea, the risks can be managed and the benefits—especially saved lives—are too great to ignore.

Some argue that monetary valuations of life, such as the Department of Transportation’s estimate of $9.2 million per person, reduce people to numbers. While such figures may guide policy decisions, they cannot capture the intrinsic worth of a human life. That said, quantifying benefits can help prioritize investments in technology and safety measures that will save lives.

We have the opportunity to use technology to benefit society and improve the lives of all Americans. With careful regulation, transparent testing, and policies that address social and economic impacts, the United States should move toward adopting self-driving cars—not because the technology is flawless now, but because the potential to save lives and reduce suffering justifies deliberate, responsible action.

A machine differs from the brain because it has a set algorithm programmed to provide an expected solution. There is no wrong solution as the only appropriate solution is already being used. Self-driving cars can help reduce accidents caused by humans since they do not have an inclination to be distracted. The machine has one job and it does it efficiently. output_text:
When Back to the Future I and II were released in the 1980s, many people were captivated by the technology shown throughout the films. Although technology has advanced greatly in the last few decades, it still has not reached the status of flying cars and floating hoverboards. As we approach 2020, the idea of having such technology no longer seems impossible. Olivier Garret stated in a Forbes article that he believed 10 million self-driving cars would be on the road by 2020, and that one out of four cars would be self-driving by 2030. Although Back to the Future II predicted flying cars in 2015, Garret’s estimate for widespread self-driving cars is not that far off.

Within the past year, companies such as Ford, Honda, BMW, and Tesla have been among the many automakers pushing for self-driving technology. This push makes sense: technology is advancing rapidly and will continue to do so as long as demand exists. Because self-driving cars are a relatively new idea and there has not been extensive research on their long-term effects, it is important to understand their advantages and disadvantages before fully adopting autonomous vehicles. Overall, I support the development of self-driving cars because their benefits—reduced human error, decreased driver distraction, and more efficient transportation—outweigh the drawbacks, provided that major concerns are addressed.

The primary advantages of self-driving cars include reduced human error, fewer distracted drivers, and increased transportation efficiency. Autonomous systems can be programmed to follow traffic laws consistently, which should reduce accidents caused by speeding, running red lights, or driving under the influence. If drivers are no longer required to control the vehicle constantly, they can safely perform other tasks during transit, increasing productivity and comfort. Finally, the coordination possible among autonomous vehicles could improve traffic flow and reduce congestion, leading to faster, more efficient commutes.

Despite these benefits, several significant disadvantages must be addressed. Jobs such as driver’s education instructors, DMV clerks, and employees of ride-sharing companies like Uber and Lyft may suffer as demand for human drivers decreases, potentially increasing unemployment in those sectors. More troubling are potential technology failures: glitches or system errors could lead to accidents that are difficult to predict or assign liability for, prompting insurance companies to raise premiums or create new, costly coverage packages for autonomous vehicles. Given how much auto insurance already costs many drivers, additional mandatory coverage could be a substantial financial burden.

Overall, self-driving cars are entering society at a rapid rate. There are both promising benefits and serious concerns, and more research is needed to address safety, employment, and insurance implications. As someone who personally dislikes driving and fears being in an accident, I would gladly use a self-driving car over a regular vehicle—provided that the technology is proven reliable and the economic and legal impacts are responsibly managed.

Despite these ongoing hurdles self-driving cars have yet to overcome, the training of their neural networks pushes forward because once the technology can be implemented, the cars can solve real-world problems. The world is on the brink of environmental disaster. Irreparable damage has already be done, but further harm can be minimized through the advancement of many technologies, self-driving cars are one. Beyond the greater fuel efficiency self-driving cars provide, there is one feature that people do not often consider. Self-driving cars do not need to be owned by an individual. They can be owned by company, such as Zip Car or Uber. Consumers can pay these companies a monthly fee to have a car available to them whenever they need it and they can also opt in to a car pool so that the car picks up others going to the same place they are. Many people drive a car to work and home again, but they do not use the car throughout the day and they certainly don't use it while they are asleep. During times of non-use, the car could be picking up other riders. This would reduce the number of cars on the road, parked on the sides of streets, and adding to the carbon footprint.

There are valid concerns to this. People worry about the loss of jobs associated with self-driving cars as taxi drivers and truck drivers may become a job of the past. However, the job market is constantly evolving. It was not long ago when cars did not exist, ergo these jobs did not exist. draft3:
Self-driving cars have arrived and are on the market even if their drivers wouldn't define them as such. Drivers are using cars that will auto park, brake if they approach an obstruction, and follow a car in front of them. They may not feel like they are self-driving as they do still require some human interaction and drivers can opt-in to such features, but when they are all used in combination, the car is in effect driving itself. Even when not in control of the wheel, these features can alert drivers to cars in their blind spot and provide a rear view when the driver is in moving backwards. Whether or not people want this future is now irrelevant; it's coming and soon people will be so used to it that they won’t remember what it was like to drive without a little backup support. Eventually, they won’t remember what it was like to drive at all, and that’s a good thing. Everyone will be safer when computers take the wheel.
	People are terrible drivers. We as humans like to believe that we can stay awake, pay attention, and maneuver an enormous hunk of metal at high speeds. The reality is that even when focused on the road, the human brain can only absorb a limited amount of its surroundings. People worry that self-driving cars are not as good as humans at detecting pedestrians or other anomalies, and they aren't as good--they're better at it. Computers have the capability to process the entire environment, not just what's in their fovea or what's left over after the brain has filtered out what it considers irrelevant. And they do it without getting drowsy or lost in their thoughts. Self-driving cars have years of clocked hours on the road. In all of those hours, only one pedestrian fatality has been caused by a self-driving car in the United States. On the other hand, 23 cyclists were killed in 2017 by human-driven cars in New York City alone (CBS New York). Clearly self-driving cars already have a better track record than their predecessors. 
	However, people should not be surrendering unmonitored control of the driving to their car just yet. There are some edges to human cognition that self-driving cars do not currently share even if they surpass it on others. One of them is the knowledge that some caution should be taken when accelerating around turns or at the peak of hills. I have been in cars that are set to regulate their speed to conditions of the road and the drivers around them, but on a nice day with no other cars in sight of the cameras, they will ramp up speed as they reach the peak of the hill without any knowledge of what may be on the other side. Due to the superior reaction times of a computer as compared to a human, the car could likely avoid any unforeseen obstruction that was just at the top and out of sight of the hill, but its passengers would not have enjoyed the abrupt stop. Even without an accident, such an abrupt stop can cause air bags to deploy or cause the body to jerk against the seatbelt and snap the collar bone. Such a need for abrupt stops would have been eliminated by a careful human driver who slowed down in anticipation of what might be lurking just beyond the crest of the hill. 
	The fact that self-driving cars are not ready to chauffeur people around en masse yet does not mean that it’s not coming though or that this reality is anything to fear. These cars just need more training, and unlike people, when one car trains, they all learn. Whether its navigating the Pittsburgh left or learning to be cautious when its camera does not detect straight road ahead, when one car learns, they all learn because they all share the technology. Thankfully, for the consumer, the transition to a self-driving car is a slow one. People will have time to adjust to the new reality and the software engineers will use this time to improve the technology. We have moved from basic cruise control to cars that will match pace with traffic. We have moved from rear-view cameras to cars that parallel park themselves. People are feeling safer about letting their car take over and when consumers are ready to give up the wheel, the car manufacturers will be there with a self-driving version. When that day comes, we’ll all be safer for it, and maybe the number of cyclists killed in New York City in a given year will finally reach zero. 
	CBSNewYork. “NYC Pedestrian Deaths Drop 32 Percent Last Year.” CBS New York, CBS New York, 8 Jan. 2018, newyork.cbslocal.com/2018/01/08/2017-pedestrian-deaths-nyc/.

output_text:
Self-driving cars are here, and I support their continued development and implementation. They have been piloted in Silicon Valley, San Francisco, New Mexico, Pittsburgh, Moscow, and other locations. Although the technology exists, autonomous vehicles are not yet part of most people’s daily lives because they cannot reliably drive in all weather conditions or handle anomalous traffic events—such as when a storm knocks out traffic lights. Despite these hurdles, developers continue to train the cars’ neural networks because, once the technology is ready, self-driving vehicles can solve real-world problems.

Autonomous cars can make travel safer and more accessible. For people with disabilities, self-driving cars could enable independent errands without relying on friends or taxis. Safety would also improve for the general population: self-driving systems can reduce traffic fatalities caused by driver inattention, fatigue, or inebriation by maintaining attention and responding more consistently than humans. These benefits follow from the cars’ ability to perceive the environment continuously and react faster than a typical human driver.

Beyond safety, self-driving cars can help reduce environmental harm. The world faces a growing environmental crisis, and reducing vehicle emissions is part of the solution. Autonomous vehicles will likely provide greater fuel efficiency through optimized driving patterns and route choices. Moreover, self-driving cars do not have to be individually owned. They can be managed by companies—similar to Zipcar or ride-hailing services—allowing consumers to pay a subscription fee for on-demand access and to opt into shared rides. Many privately owned cars remain unused for large portions of the day and night; if autonomous vehicles are shared and in use more consistently, fewer cars would be needed overall. That reduction would lower the number of cars on roads and parked on streets, shrinking the carbon footprint associated with vehicle production, maintenance, and idling.

There are valid concerns about the transition. People worry about job losses for taxi drivers and truck drivers, and those worries are real and serious. History shows that technological shifts change the job market—automobiles eliminated some roles and created others—so we should expect both job displacement and the emergence of new work sectors. Policymakers and industry must plan for retraining programs, safety nets, and new employment opportunities to ease the transition and ensure that the benefits of autonomous vehicles are broadly shared.

Change can be daunting, and mistakes during deployment would be costly. For that reason, regulators, manufacturers, and researchers should proceed cautiously: testing in diverse conditions, setting clear safety standards, and phasing in use cases where autonomous systems clearly outperform humans. With careful implementation and social supports for displaced workers, self-driving cars can increase safety, expand mobility for underserved populations, and reduce environmental impact. For these reasons, I support advancing self-driving technology while addressing legitimate economic and safety concerns.

output_text:
The growth of technology and machines has reached its peak in the 21st century. Humans have found ways to automate many tasks using high computing power and sophisticated algorithms. Driving is a passion for some and a mundane chore for others, but it has been an important skill for a long time. Recently, improvements in road infrastructure have reduced the effort required for driving. Self-driving cars could eliminate that daily task for many people, especially taxi drivers for whom driving is a job rather than a passion. Although I strongly support research in autonomous vehicles, I do not favor their widespread implementation at this time.

Many millennials, including myself, began driving at an early age. As the global economy has grown and cars have become more affordable, more people have had the opportunity to drive. Self-driving cars could greatly reduce the effort involved in driving and be beneficial to some, but redundant for others. I was born and raised in the foothills of the Himalayas, where we never had the luxury of excellent infrastructure. The deadly Himalayan curves demand intense concentration; driving there has always been and will remain a challenge. Self-driving cars have been tested and performed exceptionally well in the United States, a country known for its infrastructure and car culture. However, the United States cannot serve as the ultimate testing ground for self-driving vehicles. Their success currently relies on highly accurate mapping systems and GPS, which are not universally reliable. GPS performance can degrade in poor weather, and this limitation directly affects autonomous driving. These systems depend heavily on machine learning, and the algorithms used to train them still cannot guarantee 100 percent accuracy, which raises serious concerns in critical situations.

Another major concern is the human factor: we still need people who can operate and supervise sophisticated technology. Many individuals, particularly middle-aged and older adults, are reluctant to use advanced technology and hesitant to relinquish control to machines. This nervousness can lead to misuse or helplessness during malfunctions and may put drivers in more dangerous situations. Even if we set aside these human factors, the cost of implementing self-driving technology on a large scale is tremendous and may be financially infeasible. The expenses of deployment and the infrastructure required to support fully autonomous vehicles may outweigh the expected benefits.

Claims that self-driving cars will dramatically reduce accidents are also questionable. There have been several incidents in which autonomous vehicles were involved in crashes, showing that they do not eliminate the problem. A simple malfunction can create major hazards, and a human operator may have only seconds to recognize the issue and take control. On Himalayan roads, where drivers must maintain full attention at every curve, such malfunctions could be deadly.

In conclusion, self-driving vehicles are a promising idea, but they are constrained by factors such as infrastructure quality, GPS availability, weather conditions, human readiness, and cost. At present, widespread implementation is not feasible on a grand scale, though autonomous vehicles can be useful in controlled environments where these constraints are met.

output_text:
Self-driving vehicles will decrease fatalities, shorten commutes, reduce public-transit spending, and provide more transportation options for people with disabilities. These benefits affect virtually every person in a community because transportation touches many aspects of daily life: waking earlier to compensate for traffic, missing work because of delays, or receiving phone calls about loved ones injured in accidents. Wider adoption of autonomous vehicles will improve daily life not only for those who own them but also for those who rely on the transit systems they will change.

Currently, the U.S. Department of Transportation values a life at $9.2 million. Safer roads resulting from fewer human errors—because computers do the driving—should reduce the number and severity of collisions. Fewer collisions will lower medical and legal costs and reduce payouts by insurance companies, which can drive down insurance premiums as insurers compete. As production scales and companies streamline manufacturing and distribution, the purchase price of autonomous vehicles should fall, making them more accessible. Municipalities will also save: with a reduced need for large fleets of buses and trains, maintenance and operating costs for public transit can decline. Those municipal savings can be redirected to other public needs or result in lower taxes for residents.

A caveat is the effect on employment in public transit and taxi services. A decline in demand for those services could lead to job losses. However, new opportunities will arise: increased demand for engineers, technicians, mechanics trained to work on autonomous systems, road maintenance workers, and vehicle sales and support staff. History shows that technological shifts often displace some jobs while creating others that require different skills; proactive retraining and education programs can ease this transition and help workers move into new roles.

Supporters and opponents of self-driving vehicles both raise valid points. Critics worry about cybersecurity, system failures, and ethical decisions made by algorithms. These concerns deserve serious attention: manufacturers and regulators must prioritize rigorous testing, transparent standards, and robust security measures. Still, if a monetary value is placed on human life, there is strong moral and economic incentive to reduce traffic deaths where possible. Ongoing improvements in autonomous systems, along with reduced incidents of drunk, tired, and distracted driving, should make roads safer for everyone.

The article "Top 20 Pros and Cons Associated With Self-Driving Cars", provides an in depth look into the reasoning of whether or not we, as a society, should adopt self-driving vehicles.  Of the 20 "pros" listed, an increase in safety was likely the leading factor, but also included were the benefits in commute times and city travel, the possibility for the driver to do something productive in the time that would otherwise be used for driving, and the idea that elderly or disabled people would be able to ride self-driving cars without worry.  However, in contrast to the advantages listed in the article, the author also offered 20 disadvantages.  Many of these focused on the training involved in learning how to operate it, the high cost, security issues (both personal and cybersecurity), legal ramifications, the fact that a self-driving vehicle will not be able to appropriately respond to all situations and other technical hangups,and the financial implications caused by uprooting an old industry. 

When weighing the "pros" and "cons" in order to gauge whether adopting self-driving cars would be beneficial to society, it is important to look at the whole picture.  The "pros" are all valid, while it appears that many of the "cons" can be addressed as the self-driving vehicles further develop.  For example, the high initial cost is cited as a reason against adopting these vehicles.  However, what isn't taken into consideration in this case is the fact that new technologies typically drastically drop in price over a short amount of time.  We often hear the story of the first computers being the size of an entire room and costing hundreds of thousands of dollars at the time.  However, in a few tens of years the price of those computers came down enough that many families could afford them.  I believe a similar situation could occur with the sensor and computer package required in self-driving vehicles.  As a second example, cybersecurity has become a very hot topic recently and appears in the "cons" against these vehicles.  However, several very critical pieces of infrastructure are operated and they have been largely protected against cybersecurity attacks.  Take for example remote controlled drones currently in use by the military.  The risk of a cyber attack is definitely present, but the designers have found a way to adequately protect the devices.  Something similar could be done for self-driving vehicles.

Although several more examples supporting the idea that many of the present "cons" seen in self-driving vehicle adoption can be corrected in the future, it is unnecessary seeing that they are quite similar.  However, what is important to notice is that all of the "pros" do not seem as if they will be changing, even as the vehicles themselves improve.  Therefore I recommend the gradual adoption of self-driving vehicles into society.

output_text:
The article "Top 20 Pros and Cons Associated With Self-Driving Cars" provides an in-depth look at whether society should adopt self-driving vehicles. Of the 20 pros listed, increased safety is likely the leading benefit; others include reduced commute times, more productive use of time that would otherwise be spent driving, and greater mobility for the elderly and disabled. In contrast, the author also offers 20 disadvantages, many of which focus on the training required, high initial cost, security issues (both personal and cybersecurity), legal ramifications, the inability of self-driving vehicles to respond appropriately to every situation, other technical hang-ups, and the financial disruption caused by upending an existing industry.

When weighing the pros and cons to determine whether adopting self-driving cars would benefit society, it is important to look at the whole picture. The pros are compelling, while many of the cons seem likely to be addressed as the technology develops. For example, the high initial cost is frequently cited as a reason against adoption. However, new technologies typically drop drastically in price over a relatively short time. Early computers filled rooms and cost hundreds of thousands of dollars, yet within a few decades became affordable for many households. A similar reduction in price could occur for the sensors and computing packages required by self-driving vehicles. As a second example, cybersecurity is a prominent concern among the cons. Still, several critical pieces of infrastructure are already protected against cyberattacks. Consider remote-controlled drones used by the military: although the risk of cyberattacks exists, designers have found ways to secure them adequately. Similar protections could be developed for self-driving vehicles.

Although there are many additional examples showing that current cons can be mitigated over time, the key point is that the core pros—improved safety, greater accessibility, and productivity gains—are unlikely to disappear as the technology improves. For these reasons, I recommend the gradual adoption of self-driving vehicles into society.

The new technology of self-driving cars is decades in the making in Pittsburgh, with Carnegie Mellon first leading the way in the 1990s.  Since then the city has been a literal testing ground for companies like Uber and Argo AI, with the mayor keen on attracting (and keeping) talent at the forefront of this technology.  Reactions have long been mixed, though, even before a fatal accident in Arizona recently halted Uber automated test drives.  The strong opposition to automated vehicles in the aftermath, however, is somewhat surprising, given how much safer the technology tends to be.  Or, rather, how unsafe human drivers actually are.

A single death at the hands of an automated Uber stopped its vehicle testing, yet eighty-one percent of vehicular accidents are the result of human error.  Those in working in Oakland routinely see that this human error extends to pedestrians and cyclists in addition to human drivers.  Humans lack the reaction time to respond to unexpected changes in scenery, from a pedestrian stepping out onto Forbes to a car swerving abruptly to miss a pothole.  Human drivers are also increasingly distracted, and are choosing to distract themselves with a dizzying array of activities from personal grooming to streaming television.  Self-driving cars also solve the problem of drunk driving, as autonomous vehicles double as designated drivers.  In a city with limited taxi service and practically no commercial designated driving companies, drunk drivers find themselves tempted to drive from the Southside to the South Hills with often destructive consequences.

Accessibility and inclusion are on residents' minds, too, as the city plans to implement a rapid transit system that will make current connections faster but will leave lower income and disabled locals out of the discussion.  Fleets of self-driving cars would not only ferry drunk passengers but others without access to vehicles as well, including those in neighborhoods lacking in public transportation.  Pittsburgh's elderly and disabled, who used to be better served by paratransit services before budget cuts gutted these systems, are especially vulnerable by their lack of access.

The counter to the accessibility argument is, of course, the high cost of self-driving vehicles.  With an initial cost of more than $100,000, there is little overlap between the people who currently rely on public transportation and those who can immediately purchase self-driving vehicles.  Not many car owners could afford a $100,000 vehicle, for that matter.  This is alleviated by two considerations, though.  The cost of this technology will inevitably go down, just like the brand new CDs, Nintendos, and personal computers of our youth.  What is unaffordable now may not be in a few years.  What is less certain, though, is whether the city can shed its car-first mentality and embrace shared self-driving vehicles, a public personal transit system.

Self-driving cars will save hundreds of lives in the region each year, will virtually eliminate distracted and drunken driving, and will make the city more accessible and livable for even more Pittsburghers.  Though there is some resistance to the technology and the culture change it will bring, it isn't as new or radical as it first may seem.
          The "new" technology of self-driving cars is decades in the making here, with Carnegie Mellon first leading the way in the 1990s. Since then, the city has been a literal testing ground for companies like Uber and Argo AI, with Mayor Peduto keen on attracting (and keeping) the talent at the forefront of this technology. Car makers have been introducing automated technology in baby steps, from rear-facing cameras to park-assist features and obstacle sensors.  Reactions have long been mixed, though, even before a fatal accident in Arizona recently halted Uber automated test drives.  What is surprising is the strong opposition to automated vehicles in the aftermath, given how much safer the technology tends to be. Or, rather, how unsafe human drivers actually are. This danger is not just limited to drivers, but to everyone in our neighborhoods: pedestrians, cyclists, and bus-riders alike. The increased safety of our roads for all residents that we will see with self-driving cars is the greatest reason to advocate for their use throughout the city. 
	A single death at the hands of an automated Uber stopped its vehicle testing, yet the Center for Disease Control estimates over 33,000 Americans are killed each year by automobiles.  An alarming  81% of these vehicular accidents are the result of human error. Those working in Oakland routinely see that this human error extends to pedestrians and cyclists in addition to human drivers. Humans lack the reaction time to respond to unexpected changes in scenery, from a pedestrian stepping out onto Forbes to a car swerving abruptly to miss a pothole. Human drivers are also increasingly distracted, and are choosing to distract themselves with an obscene array of activities from personal grooming to streaming television. For those distracted and inhibited by alcohol, self-driving cars also solve the problem of drunk driving, as autonomous vehicles double as designated drivers. In a city with limited taxi service and practically no private designated driving companies, drunk drivers find themselves tempted to make the drive from the Southside to the South Hills, with often destructive consequences. 
	Accessibility and inclusion are on residents' minds, too, as the city plans to implement a rapid transit system that will make current connections faster but will leave lower-income and disabled locals on the curb. Fleets of self-driving cars would not only ferry drunk passengers but others without access to vehicles as well, including those in neighborhoods lacking in public transportation. Pittsburgh's elderly and disabled, who used to be better served by paratransit services before budget cuts gutted these systems, are especially vulnerable by their lack of access. 
	The counter to the accessibility argument is, of course, the high cost of self-driving vehicles. With an initial outlay of more than $100,000, there is little overlap between the people who currently rely on public transportation and those who can immediately purchase self-driving vehicles. Not many car owners could afford a $100,000 vehicle, for that matter. This is alleviated by two considerations, though. The cost of this technology will inevitably go down, just like the brand new CDs, Nintendos, and personal computers of our youth. What is unaffordable now may not be in a few years. What is less certain, though, is whether the city can shed its car-first mentality and embrace shared self-driving vehicles, a public personal transit system.  Uber and Lyft have revolutionized Pittsburgh's taxi scene in just a few years, after all, and those improvements to accessibility and availability will improve as the numbers of self-driving cars increase.
	Few weeks go by where out city is not making national headlines for its livability, its technology, and its overall rust-belt renaissance. Self-driving cars are currently at the vanguard of this movement, and both attract talent to the region and retain the minds trained by Carnegie Mellon and Pitt. While this has a cachet of modernity and elitism, the benefits of self-driving cars reach everyone. Indeed, pockets of our city now under served and under represented will better enjoy the benefits of our "rust belt renaissance."  When people really look at the costs of human drivers---and human errors---the choice to adapt self-driving cars throughout the city will be an easy one.

It seems that cars have always been the same: four wheels, a body, a steering wheel. They are ridiculously simple! Compare to the history of bicycles for example. If you happen to have seen those old black and white photographs of early bicycles, you will see a complex evolution, akin to those in biology! Nevertheless, something on the horizon is going to turn everything about cars upside down (well, the cars themselves will probably still remain on their four wheels, though!)

Self-driving cars are already among us fueling the debate over their good and and evil. Proponents of automated cars argue that self-driving cars will eliminate the catastrophic number of fatal car crashes because the cold-blooded machines will not be distracted by music, a passer-by, or be under the influence of alcohol and drugs. The other feature they cite is the possibility for many, or even all, cars in a certain city to communicate with one another to coordinate the speed levels, and red light waits so as to achieve the maximum flow and minimum travel times. Automated cars, they say, will allow for more cars to drive next to each other, due to their precision in maneuvering, and yet lead to less congestion. Another major advantage will be their service to the disabled and elderly people. Once a good proportion of cars on the street become self-drivers, the advocates claim that the whole society will benefit: saving lives, avoiding financial setbacks from car accidents, less fuel consumption, fewer traffic police officers on duty, etc. 

However, the opponents of the technology have some legitimate concerns. In this category there are some superficial and trivial arguments that can be refuted right away. For example, the phobia of a scenario where a car breaks down and the human driver, having forgotten how to drive, has no idea of what to do, leading to chaos in the city cannot be a real argument against self-driving cars. Already today, an average individual has no skill to get a car to start up if it all of a sudden stops in the middle of street. Such a situation rarely disrupts a whole city! Some other arguments question the limits of the technology. What if it snows? What if the road is full of potholes (such as those in Pittsburgh!)? Well, these while valid concerns do not prove the technology insufficient. There are improvements coming every day and all those conditions will be taken care of.

With the obvious criticism of the self-driving cars out of way, we arrive at some deep issues against the technology. On the infrastructure level, there is the problem of providing cyber security to such a delicate network of interconnected software that store and communicate data between cars. If, as Guardian suggests, there is a potential for hackers to manipulate such a system, then it will be a true disaster. Achieving a high level of security while possible could be prohibitively costly, overshadowing any amount of saving on gasoline.

However, the biggest challenge facing self-driving cars is their popularity. The pros listed above all are predicated on the hope that they will come in large quantities. The effect of having cars coordinate their paths will not be felt if they substitute only 2% of all the cars out there. Same holds true for safety. Have you noticed that sometimes, ironically, the safest drivers, are the very cause of some accidents? Imagine a road where the speed limit is 50, but "everyone knows that it is ok to go 60 (when the police is not around!)". Then that one guy who sticks to that 50 mph limit is the source of frustration for those behind him/her and a possible danger for everyone involved. Thus, the advantages of autonomous cars will begin to take effect only after a good portion of the society adopts them.

But with the currently high cost of these cars, and the natural public resistance to change, it seems that self-driving cars will, for the coming years, live in the margins, only triggering imagination and debates from time to time. At this point in time, we are just not ready yet to embrace them -- definitely not in large numbers.

It seems that cars have always been the same: four wheels, a body, a steering wheel. They are deceptively simple. Compare them to the history of bicycles: early black-and-white photographs show a complex evolution, almost like biological change. Nevertheless, something on the horizon will turn many assumptions about cars upside down. The vehicles will likely keep four wheels, but their operation and role in society will change dramatically.

Self-driving cars are already among us, fueling debate over their benefits and dangers. I take the position that autonomous vehicles promise important advantages but are not yet ready for widespread adoption. Proponents argue that self-driving cars will greatly reduce fatal crashes because machines are not distracted by music or passersby, nor impaired by alcohol or drugs. Another commonly cited benefit is vehicle-to-vehicle communication: cars could coordinate speeds and red-light waits to maximize traffic flow and minimize travel times. Because of their precision, automated cars could travel closer together safely, reducing congestion. Advocates also point to service for the disabled and elderly as a major social benefit. If a significant proportion of vehicles on the road were autonomous, society could gain from saved lives, reduced financial losses from accidents, lower fuel consumption, and possibly fewer traffic officers on duty.

Opponents of the technology raise legitimate concerns, although some objections are superficial and easily refuted. For example, the fear that drivers will forget how to operate a car and cause chaos if an autonomous vehicle breaks down is overblown. Today, most drivers cannot restart a stalled vehicle in the middle of the street, yet such incidents rarely disrupt an entire city. More substantive criticisms question the limits of the technology: what happens in heavy snow, or on roads full of potholes, as in Pittsburgh? These are valid concerns, but they do not prove the technology impossible. Improvements are ongoing, and engineers are addressing adverse weather and poor road conditions.

After dismissing the superficial criticisms, deeper issues remain. At the infrastructure level, securing a delicate network of interconnected software that stores and transmits data between cars is a major challenge. As The Guardian and other outlets have suggested, hackers manipulating such a system could cause disasters. Achieving a high level of cybersecurity is possible but could be prohibitively costly, potentially offsetting savings from reduced fuel use and fewer accidents.

The biggest challenge for self-driving cars, however, is adoption. The benefits advocates list—coordinated traffic flow, improved safety, and better service for those unable to drive—depend on widespread use. If autonomous vehicles make up only 2% of the fleet, those benefits will not materialize. Safety gains, in particular, require a critical mass. Consider how a single cautious driver can disrupt traffic: if the speed limit is 50 mph but most drivers go 60 when police are absent, the one driver who obeys the limit can cause frustration and create hazards. Similarly, autonomous vehicles will be most effective when a substantial portion of the driving population uses them.

Given the current high cost of these cars and the natural public resistance to change, self-driving vehicles will likely remain marginal for the coming years, provoking imagination and debate but not widespread transformation. At this point, society is not ready to embrace them en masse. In short, while autonomous cars offer promising benefits, those benefits will accrue only after we solve technical, security, and adoption challenges and after the technology becomes accessible to a broad segment of the population.

output_text:
Self-driving vehicles offer both significant advantages and serious risks. Overall, I support the development and use of autonomous cars because of the opportunities they create—provided we adopt strict safety standards and robust protections against malfunction and hacking. In this essay I will describe the main benefits of self-driving cars, then explain my concerns and why strong regulation is necessary.

One major advantage is increased mobility for people who otherwise cannot drive. Autonomous vehicles can give people with physical, mental, or emotional disabilities greater independence. They can also help elderly drivers who may have difficulty reacting quickly or sustaining long drives. In short, self-driving cars can expand access to transportation for populations who currently depend on others.

Another important benefit is improved safety through artificial intelligence. Self‑driving systems can detect risky situations and respond faster than many human drivers—examples include recognizing objects entering the vehicle’s path and executing emergency braking. Because computers can monitor many inputs continuously without distraction or fatigue, they can reduce human error, which is a major cause of accidents.

Self-driving technology also makes everyday tasks like parking easier. As parking spaces become scarcer and tighter, the uncertainty and stress of parking can be relieved when a vehicle parks itself accurately. This convenience reduces driver anxiety and saves time during errands and other routine activities.

Despite these advantages, I am not comfortable ceding full control of driving to a computer without strong safeguards. Computers can malfunction, and a failure could endanger drivers, passengers, and pedestrians. A malfunctioning system might create more dangerous situations than a human driver would have handled. My distrust is reinforced by cultural examples, such as the film 2001: A Space Odyssey, where a ship’s computer—HAL—misinterprets human actions and turns dangerous. While the film is fiction, it highlights the real fear that complex automated systems can act unpredictably.

Beyond malfunctions, autonomous vehicles are vulnerable to hacking and deliberate interference. If terrorists or malicious actors gain control of vehicle systems, the consequences could be severe. These human-driven threats mean that technical reliability alone is not enough; cybersecurity and rigorous oversight are essential.

In conclusion, self-driving cars have the potential to improve mobility, safety, and convenience, but their benefits will be realized only if we address the risks. I support the adoption of autonomous vehicles alongside strong regulation, comprehensive testing, and robust cybersecurity measures to protect users and the public.

draft1:
Self-driving cars appear to be the next wave of technology that will impact our lives, following the pattern previously exploited by the internet, cell phones, and other technological advances. The prospect of cars that autonomously drive is an attractive one for a number of positive reasons. One argument for self-driving cars could be that they would reduce car accidents and therefore save lives. 81 percent of car crashes are the result of human error; therefore, it is appealing to think that computer systems specifically designed to combat the issues associated with human error could be good alternatives. By developing specific software algorithms to respond to a myriad of situations and scenarios, self-driving cars appear to have the capability to handle any situation a human driver might encounter. Because self-driving cars would be fully automated to respond to all situations, it is comforting to think that all actions made in response to situations would lead to the best possible outcomes.
	Unfortunately, we still have a way to go before these prospects and visions about self-driving cars can truly be attained. I would like to argue that we need to spend more time considering the legal and moral dilemmas presented by self-driving cars and their actions, and how these differ from the actions taken by typical human drivers. To exemplify a possible situation in which a legal and moral dilemma might arise, I would like to use a twist on the common ethical conundrum posed by the trolley problem. For our purposes, imagine you are sitting behind the wheel of your car and driving down the street. Seemingly out of nowhere, a ball rolls into the street, followed by a child chasing after it. In the moment in which you see the child, you must make a decision as to how you will react. Your two choices consist of either attempting to brake and hope that you stop in time to not injure the child, or swerving the car to the side of the road and risk hitting a tree, thus injuring yourself. As humans in this type of situation, we would be required to react nearly instantly, therefore it is difficult to fault someone for choosing either of these two options. However, because self-driving cars are programmed in advance, we must as a society decide what the best action would be for the car to take. Would the programmer who decides which action the car should take be held responsible if the car is unable to stop in time to prevent injuring the child? Or would the programmer be held responsible if the car swerves and injures the “driver” after hitting a tree? There does not seem to be an easy answer to these questions, however, if we are going to instruct self-driving cars to act in ways that produce the best outcomes, it is necessary for us to decide what this looks like and how it is manifested in various real-world examples.
	With these questions in mind, it is important that we consider the legal and ethical dilemmas resulting from advances in self-driving car capabilities, before we are ready to implement them throughout our society.
