Imagine you are sitting behind the wheel of your car, driving down the street when seemingly out of nowhere, a ball rolls out followed by a child. As humans, we must immediately react and choose to either attempt to brake and hope that you stop in time to not injure the child, or swerve the car to the side of the road and risk hitting a tree, thus injuring yourself. Because we would be required to react nearly instantly, it is difficult to blame someone legally or ethically for choosing either of these two options. However, because self-driving cars are programmed in advance, it is believed that the car would respond in the best possible option. But what is the best option here? If the car is unable to stop in time to prevent injuring the child or if the car swerves and injures the "driver" after hitting a tree, who is at fault? Is it the programmer who wrote the algorithm which resulted in the accident? Or possibly the manufacturer who built the car? There does not seem to be an easy answer to these questions, and to further complicate such scenarios, who would be held financially responsible? Car loan comparison website Auto.Loan states "Car insurance may eventually become extinct, or at least not billed to the consumer, since eventually the computer will be making all the decisions. Perhaps the premium will be paid by the car manufacturer instead of the driver". This statement seems to suggest that the car manufacturers will be responsible for paying the costs of damages, but how many self-driving car companies would be ready to take this risk and be held financially responsible for any accident caused by their cars? More importantly, should we feel safe riding in self-driving cars if they do not have the full confidence of their manufacturers or programmers to ensure their safety?
	As a possible response to quell fears over such dilemmas, one could argue that self-driving cars could be equipped with video monitoring devices to help mandate adequate driving performance. Rather than being forced to rely on humans' memory of an accident (as in the scenario above), society would be able to study the video footage to see the causes and responses that resulted in the accident, thus ensuring that the car responded appropriately. In a similar manner, this footage could then be used to better program the software in order to make self-driving cars safer for pedestrians, and also the humans sitting behind the wheel of the car.
	With the current uncertainties in mind, it is important that we consider the legal and ethical dilemmas resulting from advances in self-driving car capabilities, before we are ready to implement them throughout our society.

output_text:
Self-driving cars appear to be the next wave of technology that will impact our lives, following the pattern set by the internet, cell phones, and other technological advances. The prospect of cars that drive autonomously is attractive for several reasons. One argument in favor of self-driving cars is that they could reduce accidents and therefore save lives: 81 percent of car crashes result from human error. It is appealing to think that computer systems specifically designed to address the problems associated with human error could serve as better alternatives. By developing software algorithms to respond to a myriad of situations, self-driving cars appear capable of handling scenarios that might overwhelm human drivers. Because these cars would be programmed to respond automatically to dangerous situations, it is comforting to imagine that their actions would often lead to the best possible outcomes.

I support the development of self-driving cars because of their potential to improve safety, but I argue that we must spend more time considering the legal and moral dilemmas they present before widespread implementation. Unlike human drivers, whose split-second decisions are difficult to judge harshly, autonomous vehicles act according to prewritten code. This raises questions about responsibility and decision-making that society has not yet resolved. Consider a variation of the trolley problem: imagine you are behind the wheel and a ball rolls into the street, followed by a child chasing after it. You must decide whether to attempt to brake and hope you stop in time or swerve to avoid the child and risk hitting a tree, injuring yourself. As humans, immediate reaction and divided attention make it hard to fault a driver for choosing either option. But if a self-driving car follows programmed instructions that result in harm—either failing to stop in time to prevent injuring the child or swerving and harming the vehicle’s occupant—who should be held responsible? The programmer who wrote the decision algorithm? The manufacturer? The owner? These questions are not easy to answer, yet they are essential if we are to instruct self-driving cars to act in ways that consistently produce the best outcomes.

Proponents of autonomous vehicles rightly emphasize their safety benefits: fewer accidents caused by drunk, distracted, or reckless driving could save thousands of lives. They also note potential gains in traffic efficiency and mobility for those unable to drive. These advantages are powerful reasons to continue developing the technology. However, the existence of these benefits does not eliminate the need for robust legal frameworks and ethical standards. Even if self-driving cars reduce overall harm, they will sometimes be forced to make agonizing choices; without clear rules about accountability and acceptable decision-making criteria, victims of those rare incidents will lack recourse, and public trust in the technology will suffer.

Therefore, before self-driving cars are implemented widely, lawmakers, ethicists, manufacturers, and the public must collaborate to define liability, transparency standards, and moral guidelines for autonomous decision-making. Only by addressing these legal and ethical dilemmas now can we ensure that the safety advantages of self-driving cars are realized equitably and responsibly.

output_text:
Self-driving cars once seemed futuristic, but they are becoming a reality. As a symbol of technological advancement, they promise increased road safety, greater accessibility, and improved transportation efficiency at lower long-term cost. Although these benefits are compelling, self-driving cars also face significant obstacles: high equipment and development costs, privacy concerns from stored personal data, and limitations of sensors in extreme weather.

I believe that self-driving cars will improve road safety and enhance mobility across the United States. First, autonomous vehicles rely on sophisticated algorithms and sensors to calculate safe stopping distances and reaction times continuously. Unlike human drivers, they do not become distracted by texting, conversations, or fatigue; removing those human errors should reduce the frequency of accidents. Second, self-driving cars can expand reliable transportation options for seniors and people with disabilities. By providing door-to-door service without requiring a human driver, these vehicles can increase independence and access to jobs, medical care, and social activities. Third, cities with limited transit infrastructure could deploy autonomous fleets to serve remote or underserved areas, improving coverage without the expense of new fixed routes.

Self-driving technology can also reduce secondary problems associated with human driving. Autonomous vehicles are less likely to engage in road rage or drive under the influence, which would allow law enforcement to focus on other priorities. If fleets are electric, they will further decrease pollution and improve energy efficiency compared with many internal-combustion vehicles. Autonomous routing and the ability to drop passengers off and park remotely would reduce the need for urban parking spaces and decrease congestion caused by on-street parking and circling for spaces.

Designers must make these systems intuitive so users do not require special training. Developers should prioritize compatibility with conventional driving expectations and create interfaces that clearly communicate vehicle behavior to passengers and other road users. This will build trust and ease adoption.

Cost remains a major barrier today: the sensors, computing hardware, and software development required are expensive. However, as computational costs fall and governments support deployment through regulation and incentives, prices should decline and adoption should broaden. Privacy and sensor limitations also require attention. Manufacturers must adopt strong data protections and transparent policies, and engineers must improve sensor robustness for adverse weather so vehicles can operate safely in extreme conditions.

In summary, self-driving cars offer substantial benefits in safety, accessibility, and efficiency, but realizing those benefits will require addressing cost, privacy, and technical challenges. With careful design, regulation, and continued technological progress, autonomous vehicles can become a practical and positive part of future transportation.

Self-driving cars will be among the most innovative technologies of the twenty-first century, and I eagerly anticipate them. Imagine getting into your car and using the ride to sightsee, have a drink, or work from home. That prospect is exciting. Autonomous vehicles promise several concrete benefits: reduced traffic congestion through platooning, fewer traffic stops and related administrative costs, lower insurance expenses, improved accessibility for people with disabilities, and new economic opportunities from the infrastructure that supports them.

One clear advantage is traffic reduction. Self-driving cars can travel in tightly coordinated groups, or platoons, maintaining consistent speeds and optimal following distances. This coordination can smooth traffic flow and reduce the stop-and-go waves caused by human driving behavior. Smoother traffic not only shortens commute times but also reduces fuel consumption and emissions, which is better for the environment.

Autonomous vehicles could also lower enforcement and administrative costs. With more reliable adherence to speed limits and traffic signals, there would likely be fewer minor traffic violations and consequently fewer traffic stops and court cases. The resulting savings in time and public resources could be substantial. Additionally, as vehicles become safer through automation, auto insurance models may change, potentially reducing premiums or shifting liability toward manufacturers and fleet operators.

Accessibility is another important benefit. Self-driving cars could give people with mobility impairments greater independence. Instead of relying on specialized paratransit or access buses, a person using a wheelchair could enter a vehicle designed for their needs and travel directly to appointments or social engagements. That increased mobility would improve quality of life and reduce pressure on limited public transportation systems.

The transition to autonomous vehicles would also create new jobs. Building and maintaining the infrastructure needed for self-driving fleets—software development, sensor manufacturing, data centers, roadway upgrades, and fleet maintenance—would generate millions of positions. Although some driving jobs may decline, displaced workers could retrain for roles in these growing sectors, such as logistics coordination, vehicle servicing, or technology support.

Of course, there are legitimate concerns. Critics worry about job loss for professional drivers, cybersecurity vulnerabilities, and how liability will be determined after an accident. These issues deserve attention. However, history shows that technological shifts often create different opportunities even as they disrupt certain occupations; for example, the rise of e-commerce created new logistics, warehousing, and IT jobs. Cybersecurity and legal frameworks can—and must—evolve alongside the technology. Engineers, policymakers, and insurers are already developing safety standards, regulations, and testing protocols to address these challenges.

In short, while self-driving cars are not without drawbacks, their potential benefits—increased safety and accessibility, reduced congestion and emissions, lower administrative costs, and significant economic opportunities—make them a transformative innovation. I will be waiting in line when they are ready to drive.

draft1:
In today's age we are always looking for the 'next thing' in terms of technological advancement. Google is one company that continuously strives to answer that call. Recently they have been doing so by researching the technology for and creating self-driving cars. While this may sound like some futuristic fabrication from Back to the Future, the technology is actually here and being tested even as this piece is being written. 
  Self-driving cars would drastically change our mobility. Human error accounts for about 81 percent of car accidents; if everyone were to transition to self-drivings cars, human error would be eliminated and accidents would be projected to drastically decrease. Traffic patterns themselves would change. The technology in the car would be able to detect its proximity to other cars and adjust its speed and positioning accordingly. This has the potential to make roadways more efficient and commute shorter. One of the most attractive aspects of a self-driving car is the convenience that they provide. The New York Times has suggested that these cars would be a great asset to individuals with disabilities; instead of relying on public transportation they would be able to operate their car as anyone else would. These cars would also be an asset to an individual who wants to get work done in the time it takes to make their commute; they could safely work on a laptop or hold a conversation without having to worry about driving. 
  At this point you're thinking, 'this seems great, sign me up!', right? Well, you might want to re-consider. Self-driving cars sound great in theory but thinking through their practical applications one runs into several road blocks (pun intended). Consider this. A person gets into their car while intoxicated. In theory, this wouldn't be a problem because the car can simply drive itself and the individual can safely ride in the back without endangering themself or others. The car comes upon a traffic signal that is not operating correctly and there is a traffic cop directly the flow of cars. The self-driving car is not equipped to deal with human signals; it does not have human judgement. Instead of being able to over-ride the autonomic function of the car the individual is incapacitated themself and so the car speeds through the signal resulting in an accident where several people are injured. Whose fault is this? The intoxicated individual? The car? The other cars involved? The answer is not clear cut here but the result is the same as if an intoxicated individual had chosen to manually drive the car.
  Humans are not perfect. But then again, neither is technology. Both may have the ability to drive a car but is one really better than the other? My response would be no. A self-driving car is an amazing feat of technology. It is a tool that can be used for many positive purposes and has the potential to change the way in which humans travel. But self-driving cars should only be that: a tool. Humans should still retain the capacity to manually drive a car should the technology glitch and fail for both their safety and the safety of others.

In today's age we are always looking for the "next thing" in technological advancement. Google is one company that continuously strives to answer that call. Recently it has pursued research into and development of self-driving cars. While this may sound like a futuristic fabrication from Back to the Future, the technology is already here and being tested as this piece is written.

Self-driving cars would drastically change our mobility. Human error accounts for about 81 percent of car accidents; if everyone were to transition to self-driving cars, human error would be greatly reduced and accidents would be projected to decline significantly. Traffic patterns themselves would change: the car’s sensors could detect proximity to other vehicles and adjust speed and positioning accordingly. This has the potential to make roadways more efficient and commutes shorter. One of the most attractive aspects of self-driving cars is the convenience they provide. The New York Times has suggested that these cars would be a great asset to individuals with disabilities; instead of relying on public transportation, they could operate their own cars like anyone else. Self-driving cars would also benefit people who want to work during their commute; they could safely use a laptop or hold a conversation without having to worry about driving.

At this point you might be thinking, "Sign me up!" But you may want to reconsider. Self-driving cars sound great in theory, but practical applications reveal several roadblocks. Consider this scenario: a person gets into their car while intoxicated. In theory this would not be a problem because the car can simply drive itself and the individual can safely ride in the back without endangering themselves or others. Then the car approaches a traffic signal that is not operating correctly, and a traffic officer is directing the flow of cars. The self-driving car may not be equipped to interpret human signals; it lacks human judgment. If the incapacitated individual cannot override the car’s autonomous function, the car might proceed through the signal and cause an accident in which several people are injured. Whose fault is this—the intoxicated individual, the manufacturer, or the other drivers involved? The answer is not clear-cut, but the result is the same as if an intoxicated person had chosen to drive manually.

Humans are not perfect, but neither is technology. Both can operate a car, but one is not categorically better than the other. My position is cautious support: self-driving cars are an amazing feat of technology and a tool that can be used for many positive purposes, but they should remain tools. Humans should retain the capacity to manually drive a car in case the technology glitches or fails—for their own safety and the safety of others.

If their child is distracted by a text, it wouldn’t matter because he/she wouldn’t need to pay attention to the road. If everyone had self-driving cars, there would be much fewer accidents, and speeding would be a thing of the past, allowing police officers more time and manpower to devote to other crimes. However, although there are many positives of self-driving cars, as with any technological advancements, there are also risks. Those with self-driving cars may need to worry about keeping their data secure from hackers who may want to know a person’s most frequently visited locations. This data could be used in targeted advertisements, or for more malicious purposes such as ascertaining the best place for a potential robbery or when the home would be empty. In adverse weather or road conditions that would provide the self-driving car with less-clear visual data, there could be a greater risk for an accident. If the occupants of the car do not pay careful attention, they may end up in a car accident. Even more alarming, if a self-driving car is on a slippery road and spins out of control, does it jerk out of the way to save a child pedestrian or the fit male pedestrian? The car wouldn’t know how to make these “gut-feeling” decisions. Additionally, if an accident does happen, where does the blame lie? A car cannot be charged with vehicular manslaughter. If the driver was not technically in control of the vehicle, they may not be responsible either. All that is left is the manufacturing company, however that doesn’t seem appropriate either. New legislation would need to be implemented to account for these possible scenarios.

Whereas some industries may see job growth, public transportation personnel would have a job reduction, as would taxi drivers. The oil and gasoline industries may also suffer, potentially harming export sales from countries that would need it. If these self-driving cars are mostly electric, many gas stations may go out of business. 

Thus, although self-driving cars may provide some benefits to society, the overall risks and potential harm is far greater. People would lose the ability to do things for themselves, others may exploit holes in the technology’s security to harm others, and industries may collapse due to the sudden shift in technology that would ultimately change how the world functions.

output_text:
Self-driving cars have both beneficial and harmful effects on society. Although their promise is appealing, the adverse effects give me reason to be cautious about embracing them as the future of automobile technology. First, consider the positive side of self-driving cars.

Self-driving cars would allow parents to feel safer about their high-school children traveling to school because the risk of speeding or accidents would be reduced. If a passenger is distracted by a text, it would matter less because the vehicle would not require constant human attention. If everyone used self-driving cars, there would likely be far fewer accidents, and speeding could become a thing of the past, allowing police officers to devote time and manpower to other crimes. These vehicles would also benefit the sick and the elderly, who would otherwise need to depend on others for transportation.

From an industrial-growth perspective, the shift toward self-driving cars would promote job growth for people skilled in technology and machine learning. A new industry would be created, and this innovation could extend to public transportation; self-driving buses and trains may soon follow.

However, although self-driving cars offer many advantages, they also carry significant risks. Owners may worry about keeping their location data secure from hackers who could learn a person’s most frequently visited places. That data could be used for targeted advertising or for more malicious purposes, such as identifying the best time to commit a robbery. In adverse weather or on unclear roads, reduced sensor performance could increase the risk of accidents. If occupants are not paying careful attention, they may be unable to intervene when intervention is needed.

Even more alarming are the moral and legal dilemmas that autonomous vehicles create. If a car loses control on a slippery road and must choose between harming a child pedestrian or an adult pedestrian, how should it decide? A vehicle cannot make “gut-feeling” decisions in the way humans do. And if an accident occurs, who is to blame? A car cannot be charged with vehicular manslaughter, and if the human occupant was not technically in control, responsibility is ambiguous. Assigning blame solely to the manufacturer also seems inadequate. New legislation will be needed to address these scenarios and to allocate liability appropriately.

While some industries may see job growth, others will suffer job losses—public transportation workers and taxi drivers, for example. The oil and gasoline industries could decline as electric autonomous vehicles become more common, which could harm economies that rely on fuel exports. If most self-driving cars are electric, many gas stations may go out of business.

In sum, although self-driving cars may provide important benefits, the overall risks and potential harms give me reason to be skeptical. People could lose certain self-sufficiency, malicious actors could exploit security weaknesses, and entire industries may collapse under a sudden technological shift. A measured approach—promoting the benefits while addressing security, ethical, and legal challenges—is essential before wholesale adoption.

output_text:
As technology continues to progress at a rapid pace, innovators have begun to explore the possibility of self-driving cars. A self-driving car sounds like a child’s fantasy of a futuristic dystopia. However, self-driving cars are no longer a fantasy, and the impact these vehicles may have on society is immense. They evoke thoughts of pure leisure while commuting: with a self-driving car there would be no need to be stressed behind the wheel because something else would be taking care of the driving. A self-driving car could also help limit accidents by removing the human-error aspect of driving; these cars would be programmed to avoid collisions and obey traffic laws.

This, however, is an idealistic way of thinking. Self-driving cars bring their fair share of negative impacts. While they may reduce certain human errors, they could introduce other types of human-caused mistakes through their programming. Algorithms can be miscalculated, sensors or hardware can fail, and software updates can introduce unforeseen bugs, resulting in risks similar to—or even exceeding—those posed by an average human driver. Beyond programming faults, self-driving systems raise ethical and judgment concerns: it is not clear how an algorithm should weigh competing harms in an emergency. There are also cybersecurity risks—vehicles connected to networks could be vulnerable to hacking—and social impacts such as job displacement for professional drivers.

The risks of self-driving cars are too great to ignore when considering their implementation. One argument in favor of them is that they will not be distracted drivers because they follow algorithms. While it is true human drivers face many distractions, they retain instincts and judgment that machines cannot fully replicate. Consider a scenario in which a self-driving car is traveling steadily past a grocery store when a shopping cart suddenly rolls into its path and there is not enough time to stop. An algorithm might swerve to avoid the cart, but what if a pedestrian is crossing the street right next to it? How would the self-driving car determine whether it is better to brace for impact with the cart to avoid hitting the person? It is not clear that a self-driving car would have the instant judgment and instincts needed to process that decision in milliseconds. A human driver, on the other hand, might be capable of making a conscious decision to protect the pedestrian.

While this is only one scenario among thousands, it illustrates that self-driving cars lack the human instinct and moral judgment that no current algorithm can fully reproduce. Given these unknowns and the range of high risks—technical, ethical, and social—it is prudent and safest to avoid rushing the widespread implementation of self-driving cars, since their potential negative outcomes do not yet outweigh their benefits to society.

As with the advent and introduction of any new technology, there are pros and cons that arise with the implementation of that technology. This is also true with autonomous, or self-driving, cars. With the testing of self-driving cars currently happening in several cities across the United States, the debate on whether or not autonomous cars are safer than those driven by humans has come to the forefront of the public health and wellness debate in the media. Many cars already come equipped with some of the basic technology - autonomous breaking, self-parking, or sensors that detect close objects, so we may be closer to the future than we think.

There are several pros for the use of self-driving cars, and one of the biggest is safety. While a small percentage of car accidents could be caused by mechanical failures, the majority are caused by a distracted driver. A computer driver would take the human element factor out of the equation and remove most of the danger just by being a computer. Computers make for the ideal driver as they will not get tired, lose their eyesight, get distracted, imbibe in drugs or alcohol, etc. Computers also use specific algorithms to calculate speed, stopping distance, and other collected information that help to dramatically decrease the chance of an accident. 

In addition to safety, there are many other positive side-effects to autonomous driving. Self-driving cars could help with traffic optimization, as the cars could communicate with each other to help identify traffic problems and their specific algorithms and sensors could help to reduce commute times while also allowing more cars on the road with less traffic. People with disabilities would have more options at their disposal. Police could focus less time on traffic stops and more time on other crimes. People would have more time to do other thigns with less parking and DMV headaches for drivers. The benefits are endless. 

With all the good things that come with technology, there are bound to be some bad things, as well. One of the biggest downfalls anticipated with self-driving cars would be their affect on the economy. Not only is the cost of implementing the new technology more expensive than most Americans can afford, it could also eliminate a lot of jobs in transportation. It is also expected that since most self-driving cars are powered by computers, that they would be electric cars, further putting a strain on the gas industry. Reliance on technology could make a driver's license, insurance, the DMV, and other driving related vendors obsolete. The benefits of self-driving cars would also depend upon more people opting into the option. 

Other downfalls to autonomous cars would be the flaws with technology itself. A computer cannot detect a road block or read human hand signals, so road construction would be an obstacle. The sensors are located on the outside of the vehicle, so severe weather can damage them. Computers attract hackers and software requires user information, so there is always a chance for breach of security. Another risk would be the technology failure where a person must take over the controls - what if it is someone that has never driven their own car before? What if the GPS goes down? How will a person be able to get to where they need to go?

While a completely autonomous driving society is probably a ways off, society certainly seems to be moving towards it. I do believe that self-driving cars would be a benefit in many ways - safety, inclusion, environment, and time/traffic - but I think we have some questions that need to be answered before we get there. While there may be jobs that are lost in transportation, there will be jobs that are created with the technology and manufacturing of these vehicles. The inclusion of people with disabilities, the elderly, and those that do not have a license is a powerful tool for those that were previously reliant on others. In my opinion, self-driving cars will get over the hump and be a part of the near future, at least in a small realm.

output_text:
As with the advent of any new technology, pros and cons arise with its implementation. This is also true of autonomous, or self-driving, cars. With testing currently happening in several U.S. cities, the debate over whether autonomous vehicles are safer than human-driven cars has moved to the forefront of public discussion. Many vehicles already include basic technologies—autonomous braking, self-parking, and sensors that detect nearby objects—so we may be closer to this future than we think. Overall, I believe self-driving cars offer substantial benefits, particularly for safety and accessibility, but important questions about economics, reliability, and security must be answered before widespread adoption.

One of the biggest advantages of autonomous vehicles is safety. While a small percentage of accidents are caused by mechanical failures, the majority are the result of driver error or distraction. A computer driver eliminates many human factors that contribute to crashes: it will not become fatigued, lose focus, drink alcohol, or use drugs. Autonomous systems use algorithms to calculate appropriate speeds, stopping distances, and responses to sensor data, which can dramatically reduce the likelihood of accidents.

Beyond safety, autonomous driving offers other significant benefits. Self-driving cars could improve traffic flow by communicating with one another to identify and respond to congestion; their algorithms and sensors could reduce commute times and allow more vehicles on the road with less delay. People with disabilities would gain greater mobility and independence. Law enforcement could spend less time on traffic stops and more time addressing other crimes. Drivers would face fewer parking hassles and reduced interactions with the DMV. These advantages could improve quality of life for many.

Despite these benefits, there are valid concerns. One major downside is the potential economic impact. Implementing advanced autonomous systems is costly and may be out of reach for many consumers initially. Widespread adoption could also displace transportation jobs, from truck drivers to taxi operators. If most autonomous vehicles are electric, the traditional gas industry could face additional strain. Reliance on automation might render some existing services—driver’s licenses, certain insurance models, and DMV operations—less central. Additionally, the societal benefits of autonomous driving depend on broad participation; if only a small segment of the population opts in, gains will be limited.

There are also technical and security challenges. Autonomous systems may struggle with unusual situations: recognizing temporary roadblocks, interpreting human hand signals from construction workers, or functioning in severe weather that damages external sensors. Computers are vulnerable to hacking, and software often requires user data, raising privacy and security concerns. Another risk is the need for human takeover in emergencies—what if the person asked to take control has never driven? What if GPS or communications fail? These scenarios highlight the need for robust fail-safes and clear protocols.

Some critics argue that the risks and costs outweigh the potential benefits and that we should delay deployment until the technology is perfect. While caution is warranted, delaying progress indefinitely ignores the immediate potential to save lives and increase mobility for underserved populations. A balanced approach—continued testing, phased rollouts, strong regulatory standards, and investments in cybersecurity and workforce transition programs—can mitigate risks while allowing society to benefit from the technology.

While a completely autonomous driving society may still be some years away, we are clearly moving in that direction. I believe self-driving cars will be beneficial in many ways—improving safety, inclusion, the environment, and efficiency—but several important questions must be addressed first. Although some transportation jobs may be lost, new jobs will be created in manufacturing, technology, and maintenance. Greater inclusion for people with disabilities, the elderly, and those without licenses would be a meaningful social gain. In my view, autonomous vehicles will overcome current hurdles and become part of the near future, at least in limited and regulated contexts.

This article raised points I had not considered before. I agree that self-driving cars could reduce the number of accidents caused by human error. Too many heartbreaking stories involve drivers under the influence causing death or serious injury. One might expect incident rates to fall with the popularity and affordability of ride-sharing apps like Uber and Lyft, which make it easy to get from point A to point B without driving. Yet many people still take the risk of driving themselves. Self-driving cars could remove the need to rely on intoxicated people to make the responsible choice to use a designated driver.

One significant con is the question of liability when something goes wrong. If a child runs into the road and an autonomous vehicle fails to detect and stop in time, who is to blame? Some will argue that a human driver might have seen the child in their periphery and anticipated the danger, potentially preventing the accident. Others will seek to blame the manufacturer for sensor or software failures, or argue that the human in the vehicle should have remained attentive. These are difficult legal and ethical questions that we must resolve before fully transitioning to autonomous vehicles. Clear regulations must define when a manufacturer is accountable and when the human occupant retains responsibility.

A related concern is whether drivers would remain attentive if they rely on automation. If people assume the car will always handle every situation, they may become complacent, reducing the human ability to respond in emergencies. Traffic conditions are complex and sometimes require split-second, context-sensitive decisions that humans currently make well. For these reasons, I do not believe we should entirely replace human drivers with autonomous systems right now. Instead, we should pursue a hybrid approach: use self-driving technology to reduce accidents caused by impairment and inattention, but require drivers to remain vigilant and ready to take control when necessary.

In rebuttal to the liability and detection concerns, it is important to note that technology and policy can mitigate many of these risks. Manufacturers can continue improving sensors and algorithms, and regulators can establish rigorous testing, safety standards, and clear liability frameworks. Mandatory monitoring systems could ensure drivers stay engaged when required, and fail-safes can be developed to handle edge cases. While these solutions are not perfect, they show that the drawbacks of self-driving cars are addressable rather than insurmountable.

In conclusion, self-driving cars offer significant benefits in reducing human-error accidents, particularly those involving impaired drivers. However, they should not entirely replace human drivers at this time. Drivers should remain responsible for operating their vehicles safely unless a clear manufacturing defect is demonstrated, and regulations should define manufacturer liability where appropriate. A cautious, hybrid transition—combining improved autonomous technology with continued human attentiveness and robust legal frameworks—best balances safety and practicality.

output_text:
There has been much discussion recently about self-driving cars. Many people think that putting self-driving vehicles on the road would be a bad idea. They worry about liability in the case of accidents and about job loss for truck drivers and others employed in the automotive industry. However, self-driving cars are the way of the future: the benefits of deploying them widely outweigh the drawbacks. Autonomous vehicles will benefit society in multiple ways and help advance technology and safety.

There are many ways the advent of self-driving cars will be beneficial. For example, driver distraction is one of the leading causes of motor vehicle accidents in the United States. If a reliable computer system controlled the vehicle, it would not become distracted and could maintain continuous attention to the driving environment, substantially reducing accidents. Fewer accidents would also reduce medical, legal, and repair costs associated with collisions; those savings could be redirected toward infrastructure, public transportation, or other social needs. Many modern cars already include features that resemble early stages of autonomy—object-detection sensors, lane-keeping assistance, and autonomous braking—so the step to fully autonomous driving is an extension of existing technology rather than an entirely new leap. These existing systems provide both a technical foundation and real-world data that can improve safety as fully autonomous systems are developed.

Critics raise valid concerns, and these should be addressed through regulation and design. Some worry that drivers will lose the skills needed to operate a vehicle and could be unable to take control in an emergency. Requiring periodic re-certification—such as an annual test of driving skills and emergency procedures—would help ensure that drivers remain competent to intervene when necessary. Others fear software glitches or malicious attacks. Thorough testing, secure software-development practices, redundancy in critical systems, and regularly updated safety certifications can reduce those risks. Regarding economic effects, job displacement in driving-related fields is a real issue, but history shows that technological shifts typically create different kinds of employment even as some jobs decline. Policymakers can ease the transition with retraining programs and policies that promote new jobs in vehicle maintenance, fleet management, infrastructure, and technology development.

Self-driving cars are the subject of much scrutiny, and people have strong opinions on both sides. Nonetheless, when properly regulated, tested, and deployed, autonomous vehicles can increase safety, reduce costs associated with accidents, and spur technological and economic opportunities. I hope this op-ed clarifies the potential benefits and persuades readers to consider supporting responsible adoption of self-driving cars.

output_text:
Self-driving cars seem to be a technology of the future, and as reports of them on the streets appear increasingly in the media, their arrival in society is becoming evident. Several groups oppose the use of self-driving cars, citing increased fatalities, higher costs, and unresolved legal questions. Nevertheless, manufacturers continue to test this technology, and the benefits of self-driving cars appear to outweigh the negatives. I argue that, despite legitimate concerns, self-driving cars have the potential to make transportation safer, more efficient, and ultimately less costly.

The first reason many groups oppose self-driving cars is fear of an increased chance of accidents. The typical argument is that if no one controls the wheel, the computer will make a mistake and could cause an accident or even kill someone. Although this perspective is frightening and recent incidents in the media are alarming, it is overly pessimistic to assume the worst. The fact is that 81 percent of car crashes are caused by human error; widespread implementation of self-driving cars would eliminate most crashes caused by human mistakes. Crashes caused by autonomous systems are likely to be significantly less frequent than those caused by humans, because computers do not drive while distracted, intoxicated, or fatigued and can react faster in many situations.

A second common concern is the cost of implementing the technology necessary for self-driving cars. Admittedly, the software, sensors, and research required will add to the vehicle’s sticker price. But what is often overlooked is the significant reduction in insurance and healthcare costs that could follow. The U.S. Department of Transportation values a human life at $9.2 million, and insurance companies use similar estimates when calculating costs related to fatal crashes. If autonomous systems reduce crash frequency and severity, the financial burden of accidents—medical expenses, lost productivity, and liability—will decline, lowering insurance premiums and societal costs. Another way costs are saved is through the time made available to individuals riding in self-driving cars. Time is valuable, and if passengers can use travel time to work, rest, or otherwise be productive instead of focusing on driving, that represents an economic benefit that offsets some of the technology’s upfront costs.

A final objection concerns legal precedent and liability when a self-driving car causes an accident. If such incidents occur, questions about who is responsible and who must pay for damages will arise. This is a pressing issue as we push the boundaries of technology. However, avoiding innovation because of legal uncertainty would prevent the development of solutions to present and future problems. Lawmakers, regulators, manufacturers, and insurers can create new frameworks that assign responsibility appropriately and incentivize safety. If self-driving cars can deliver a safer, more productive, and more cost-efficient transportation system, it is in society’s interest to develop and refine the legal and technical systems that will allow the technology to succeed.

output_text:
Up with Self-Driving Cars and Down with the Gasoline Industry

I am in favor of adopting self-driving cars because they promise to eliminate many accidents and reduce reliance on the gasoline industry, which causes excessive pollution and is becoming obsolete. Unlike human drivers, the computers that operate autonomous vehicles do not become distracted. The average human attention span is only eight seconds, so a person might not respond quickly enough to a deer in the road or a bicyclist crossing an intersection. Human error contributes to 94% of traffic accidents; many of those accidents result from drunk or distracted driving.

Self-driving cars can prevent many accidents, particularly those caused by distracted drivers. Distracted driving is a leading cause of crashes: people often talk or text on their cell phones, eat and drink, converse with passengers, or fiddle with music controls while driving. Children, pets, and activities such as applying makeup can also divert a driver’s attention. Autonomous systems, by contrast, continuously monitor the environment and respond faster than most humans can.

Other causes of accidents—speeding, drunk or reckless driving, running red lights or stop signs, unsafe lane changes, wrong-way driving, road rage, and drowsy driving—can be greatly reduced or eliminated by self-driving technology. Autonomous vehicles are designed to obey traffic laws consistently, maintain safe following distances, and react to hazards without fatigue or impaired judgment.

Most self-driving cars will likely be electric, and electric vehicles are better for the environment. They are more energy-efficient and generate less pollution than internal combustion engines. Electric cars are typically quieter and smoother compared with gas-operated vehicles. They also have lower operating costs—electricity often costs a fraction of gasoline—and require less maintenance because they do not need oil changes. Because electric cars lack tailpipes, widespread adoption would improve air quality and reduce exposure to harmful chemicals such as benzene, which is linked to cancer. Gasoline relies on finite fossil fuels; transitioning to electric vehicles reduces that dependence and helps preserve resources.

Critics raise legitimate concerns that deserve consideration. Some worry about job losses in the gasoline and auto-repair industries, or among drivers who earn a living transporting others. However, many mechanics can be retrained to service electric vehicles, and new jobs will emerge in software, battery production, charging infrastructure, and vehicle maintenance specific to electric drivetrains. When the nation shifted from horse-drawn carriages to automobiles, people adjusted and the economy evolved; we can do the same now. Others worry about safety risks unique to autonomous vehicles, such as software failures or hacking. These risks call for robust regulation, rigorous testing, and strong cybersecurity measures—not rejection of the technology altogether.

In short, self-driving electric cars offer significant safety and environmental advantages. While transition challenges and new risks must be managed through training programs, regulations, and technological safeguards, the benefits—fewer accidents, cleaner air, and lower operating costs—make the shift away from gasoline-powered vehicles both necessary and desirable.

A second benefit is increased mobility and accessibility. Self-driving cars could provide independence to those who cannot drive due to age, disability, or medical conditions, expanding their ability to work, socialize, and access services. This increased autonomy would reduce reliance on caregivers and public transportation schedules, improving quality of life for many people who currently face mobility barriers.

A third benefit is better efficiency and environmental impact. Autonomous vehicles can optimize routes, reduce unnecessary acceleration and braking, and improve traffic flow, which would lower fuel consumption and emissions. Reduced congestion also shortens commute times, improves productivity, and can decrease the stress associated with driving.

Many of the common objections to self-driving cars are solvable. For instance, critics point out that autonomous vehicles may not interpret human-directed traffic signals when a light is out and an officer or flagger is directing traffic. This can be addressed by improving software to recognize human gestures and temporary traffic controllers, and by designing interfaces that allow the car to query a human passenger when ambiguity arises. Similarly, concerns about GPS inaccuracies and sensor failures can be reduced through redundant systems, better mapping, and industry standards for hardware and software reliability.

Economic and social costs—such as job displacement in driving professions—are real but manageable through policy responses. Retraining programs, phased implementation, and new employment opportunities in vehicle maintenance, software development, and fleet management can offset job losses. The transition to autonomous vehicles should be accompanied by thoughtful regulation, worker protections, and investments in education to ensure that the benefits are widely shared.

Self-driving cars present more benefits than costs and therefore should be integrated into the transportation systems of the future. If more information is shared about autonomous technology and how its potential costs can be managed and prevented, more people will be willing to make the switch and enjoy a safer, easier life. The decision is not merely whether the technology exists—the decision is whether society is ready to embrace the future and manage its challenges responsibly.

Some argue that self-driving cars are still in development and that any error could cost human lives or money. output_text:
Society is constantly growing and adapting with new technology. Products that would have been completely unfathomable years ago are becoming available to the public today. One such futuristic concept now being tested is the self-driving car. This technology is appealing for several reasons: increased accessibility, a perceived reduction in accidents, and predicted traffic efficiency. However, there are also many drawbacks to integrating self-driving cars into society: high cost, potential software or sensor malfunctions, future driving incompetence, and the loss of jobs that involve driving. Despite possible benefits, the cons of self-driving cars outweigh the advantages they could bring to society.

According to the article, a primary motivation for self-driving cars is that they will greatly reduce accidents; yet many factors must be perfect for that outcome to occur. Passengers still need basic knowledge of how to operate the vehicle if something goes wrong, and human error remains a possibility. Poor weather can compromise sensors such as lidar and cameras, so passengers must be ready to take control in case of malfunction, which reintroduces the risk of human-driven accidents. Self-driving systems also cannot be programmed with every local driving law, road hazard, or construction rerouting; GPS and mapping programs are not always accurate, and manual driving can often correct for that. There is no way to guarantee that self-driving cars will drastically reduce accidents. They would also complicate legal liability when crashes do occur: who is at fault—the vehicle, the manufacturer, the software developer, or the passenger?

Self-driving cars also create social and economic concerns. Most of the general public may be unable to afford them initially. Current estimates for the engineering, sensors, and power systems put costs far beyond the reach of many consumers. To achieve meaningful safety or efficiency benefits, automated vehicles would need widespread adoption, which seems impractical if costs remain high. Even if implementation becomes widespread, many jobs that rely on driving would disappear. Auto manufacturers, driver education companies, personal injury lawyers, professional drivers, and segments of the gasoline industry could struggle to find work. Significant layoffs in these sectors would negatively affect unemployment and the broader economy.

A fair rebuttal is that self-driving cars could increase mobility for people who cannot drive, reduce human-error accidents in ideal conditions, and improve traffic flow through coordinated driving. These are legitimate advantages: autonomous vehicles can offer independence to the elderly and disabled, and they can, in controlled environments, maintain consistent speeds and following distances that improve traffic efficiency. Nonetheless, these benefits depend on near-perfect technology, regulatory frameworks, and equitable access. Without affordability, robust fail-safes, clear liability rules, and retraining programs for displaced workers, the theoretical advantages will not materialize for most people and may create greater social harms.

Technology is a great asset when applied where it is truly needed and responsibly implemented. Do we want a society that gradually loses the ability to operate a car manually—one that, in the event of a software glitch or widespread recall, could be left without reliable transportation because of overreliance on automation? Do we want an innovation that contributes to the destruction of many jobs without plans to mitigate the consequences? Given the current technological, legal, and economic uncertainties, the drawbacks of self-driving cars currently outweigh their potential benefits.

Technology is evolving every day and plays a huge role in our lives in the 21st century. It is almost like living in a sci‑fi movie. From our phones and TVs to our refrigerators, everything seems to be “smart,” and soon we may be able to add cars to that list. A world with self‑driving cars might not be far off: companies such as Google have been testing cars that drive themselves in certain states. But are these cars really more beneficial for society? Some argue that self‑driving cars would be safer than human drivers and would therefore reduce car accidents tremendously. I disagree. I believe these cars would not be more beneficial because they are costly—not only in money, but potentially in lives as well.

Each year roughly 33,000 people die in car accidents, and many believe self‑driving cars could help solve this problem. Accidents are often caused by human error, such as distraction. Supporters claim that autonomous cars, run by computers, cannot become distracted and would thus lower the probability of fatal crashes. These cars operate with complex algorithms that allow them to detect their surroundings and respond faster, which could improve stopping times. Given estimates that place the value of a human life at around $9.2 million, proponents argue that fewer accidents would save millions of dollars.

All of this sounds promising, but self‑driving cars could do more harm than good. First, these vehicles would be costly in many ways. Accidents caused by human error might decrease, but accidents caused by software or sensor failures are still possible and could also cost lives. In cases of accidents involving autonomous vehicles, who would be to blame? Some might point to the manufacturer, others to the software developer; fatal crashes could trigger long, expensive, and exhausting legal battles for the people involved. These cars would also threaten many jobs in the transportation sector. Taxi drivers, delivery drivers, and many others could lose their livelihoods because of automation, which would have serious economic consequences for those workers and their families. Widespread adoption of autonomous vehicles could also disrupt the insurance industry if the demand for traditional car insurance declines.

Given our heavy reliance on technology, self‑driving cars may seem attractive at first glance. But technologies that improve one aspect of life can severely diminish the livelihoods of others. Self‑driving cars pose safety risks and could damage the job market. For these reasons, we should not embrace them uncritically. Say no to self‑driving cars.

Although there are several risks associated with self-driving cars, including the inability of operating safely in a variety of weather conditions and traffic situations, we still do not know whether these risks are higher than those associated with human drivers, such as distraction, drinking, and sleeping during driving. In other words, there is always a risk when driving a car, regardless of the use of the self-driving technology. When there is no evidence that self-driving cars cause more accidents, it is reasonable and more important to promote self-driving cars which can create a better transportation opportunity for people having a hard time driving and using other means of transportation. 
	Furthermore, self-driving cars can minimize the risks mentioned above through iterative data collection and analyses on their safety technology. One of the advantages of self-driving cars is that, when an accident occurs, analysis of data can reveal the cause of the accident in a reliable way. This means that the algorithm will be improved continuously and therefore a new algorithm will likely prevent a similar accident in the future. It is almost impossible to detect the exact cause when an accident occurs due to a human error, partly because human memory is sometimes inconsistent and is not perfect. Therefore reliable evidence on how and why the accident occurred would not be provided, making it difficult to understand how to prevent similar accidents in the future. Even when we detect factors that caused the accident, the immediate improvement would not happen. A variety of educational programs and policy changes have been implemented to prevent car accidents, but such changes require much more time to prove their actual impact than technological improvement given to self-driving cars. 
	To summarize, I believe that introducing self-driving cars can provide new opportunities for those having difficulty driving and using other transportation. It also minimizes risks associated with the self-driving technologies in the future as we collect driving data, which would decrease the number of car accidents and therefore offer a better experience for people.

Technological advances have made our society more convenient and efficient in many areas, including social networking, shopping, and personalized streaming services. For decades we have imagined applying new technologies to automobiles; today, sophisticated algorithms are being tested to create safer self-driving cars. Although relying on autonomous driving raises concerns about safety and new types of accidents, I believe self-driving technology can expand mobility for people with difficulties and allow rapid, data-driven improvements that reduce future risks.

First, self-driving cars can benefit not only ordinary drivers but also people who cannot drive independently—such as those with disabilities, pregnant women, the elderly, and others with mobility challenges. These individuals often must rely on public transportation or assistance for shopping, travel, and commuting, which is especially difficult where public transit is limited or in crowded urban areas. Autonomous vehicles can offer a safer, more personalized transportation option for people who currently have few alternatives. While self-driving systems face challenges operating safely in varied weather and traffic conditions, we cannot assume those risks exceed those posed by human drivers, who may be distracted, intoxicated, or drowsy. Because driving always involves risk, it is reasonable to introduce self-driving cars as an additional option that can improve access to transportation for those who need it.

Moreover, the risks associated with autonomous vehicles can be reduced through data collection and analysis. When an accident occurs in a self-driving car, rich sensor and system data can reveal the precise causes, enabling faster algorithmic fixes and software updates that help prevent similar incidents in the future. By contrast, determining the fine-grained causes of human-caused accidents is often difficult, and even when causes are identified, behavioral interventions, education, or policy changes typically take much longer to produce measurable effects.

In conclusion, introducing self-driving cars can expand transportation opportunities for people who have difficulty driving and, through continuous data-driven improvements, can reduce the likelihood of future accidents—ultimately providing a safer and more accessible transportation experience.

The use of self-driving cars may also have a negative effect on the economy because it eliminates many jobs such as taxi drivers, personal injury lawyers, and drivers ed instructors. However, the advantages in safety, efficiency, and accessibility of self-driving cars outweigh these possible risks and demonstrate that self-driving cars would be a very beneficial addition to our society.

Safety is the most notable advantage of self-driving cars. Research shows that 81 percent of car crashes are due to human error. Many factors can lead to unsafe driving for humans, such as distractions, driving under the influence of drugs or alcohol, and speeding. Since computers are not affected by these factors and instead use precise algorithms to drive, this greatly reduces the risk of accidents. Self-driving cars, however, have some disadvantages that human driven cars do not, such as risk of malfunction in certain weather conditions and inability to follow human traffic signals. There is also a question of what the driver may have to do if the technology malfunctions. However, these are all situations that occur infrequently. The situations in which self-driving cars would save lives occur much more frequently, showing that the safety advantages outweigh the possible risks.

Self-driving cars also have many advantages when it comes to efficiency in cost and time. With fewer accidents on the road, money will be saved for insurance and healthcare. Drivers are also able to use time on the road to complete other tasks since they don’t have to focus on the road. In large groups, self-driving cars participate in platooning. The cars are able to communicate with each other, which helps to reduce traffic and hazardous situations on the road. This saves time, gasoline, and also allows cars to travel at higher speeds, further saving driving time. Overall, the efficiency of self-driving cars reduces traffic and makes the road safer.
	
Finally, the accessibility of self-driving cars gives access to transportation to those who cannot drive cars themselves, such as disabled and elderly individuals. Disabled individuals often rely on others for transportation or have to use public transportation, greatly inhibiting their mobility. Access to self-driving cars would allow these individuals to travel in a more convenient way, granting them more freedom. This is true for elderly individuals as well. People often worry about allowing their elderly parents drive at a certain age, concerned about their safety but not wanting to take away their independence. With self-driving cars, this would not be an issue.
	
Though the technology for self-driving cars is not completed yet, these cars demonstrate how technology increases safety, efficiency, and accessibility. If their development is successful, the use of these cars will have a positive impact on the transportation industry and society in general.

draft1:
I marvel at the ingenuity of our scientists and engineers in bringing us so close to the possibility of self-driven vehicles. 

Furthermore, I am a free spirit who cherishes her independence, including the freedom to go where she pleases when she pleases, without having to rely on other people, their schedules, their preferences and especially their notions about what I, as a woman of a certain age, should or should not be doing. All this makes me deeply empathetic toward seniors and the disabled who may not be able to drive, and for their sakes, I can deeply appreciate the value of a self-driven vehicle.

Moreover, since self-driven cars are likely to be electric, they could mean the end of our reliance on fossil fuels,  something deeply to be desired by all of us who are worried about the future of our polluted planet.

And yet, I find that something deeper, perhaps even less rational, but nonetheless ineluctable, keeps me from endorsing self-driven, at least for the present.

We seem happy to cede increasing portions of our lives to computers. In cases where computers are absolutely better than us at certain things, e.g. the analysis of large quantities of crucial health-related data, there can be no quarrel with their exclusive use. But 
I fear we may be in danger of losing too many of our everyday skills to them, in areas where they are not particularly better than we are. Driving is a case in point. You may ask what is so valuable about this skill. And I grant that for many, driving is routine, perhaps even tedious. Even so, there is a whole parcel of skills that are ancillary to driving per se. I mean the ability to be attentive and alert in a dynamic situation, the ability to be with others and jointly negotiate traffic so that all who use the streets, including cyclists and pedestrians, are safe and minimally inconvenienced. In our increasingly atomized world, these are not skills and capacities we can afford to lose. More is at stake than we might imagine. 

And then, there is the questions of safety. We all know that computer algorithms are not infallible, that programs can be infected with viruses, that hardware can malfunction, that computers are at the mercy of hackers. A human driver has a repertoire of skills to call upon in the event of unforeseen situations: an accident, bad weather, a terrorist attack. As far as I am aware, computer algorithms that function in very dynamic situations without human intervention are not that plentiful. What then would a rider do, especially if they are not tech savvy, in a complex and fluid situation?  You may argue that we are everyday and everywhere rendered helpless by such things as drunk or inattentive motorists, with dire consequences that are clear from the statistics about accidents. I cannot disagree. And yet, I find I must stand my ground. I ask you to imagine a situation where your self-driving car cannot adapt in time, nor can the cars of anyone else, and you're all,  every single one of you, headed for disaster with no recourse, where at least if you were at the wheel you could have attempted some solution. It is this removal of human agency that I worry about. This helplessness that is not natural human vulnerability but thralldom to machines. Unlike human vulnerability, which 
can be the source of virtues like compassion and pity, this thralldom is not generative, it is lifeless in the way of an inorganic thing. I do not wish to be thus reduced.

Where computers are absolutely better than us, e.g. in the analysis of large quantities of crucial data, say health-related  data, there can be no quarrel with their exclusive use. But I fear we may be in danger of losing too many of our everyday skills to them. Driving is a case in point. 
	You may ask what is so valuable about this skill. And I grant that for many, driving is routine, perhaps even tedious. Even so, there is a whole parcel of skills that are ancillary to driving per se. I mean the ability to be attentive and alert in a dynamic situation, the ability to be with others and jointly negotiate traffic so that all who use the streets, including cyclists and pedestrians, are safe and minimally inconvenienced. In our increasingly atomized world, these are not skills and capacities we can afford to lose. More is at stake than we might imagine. 
          Some claim that self-driven cars would obviate the need for public transportation, seeing this as something to recommend them. I disagree. Apart from the jobs lost, which I think most would see as a negative, there is the potential loss of public space, which after all, is what a bus, a subway car, or a train is. Like cities, where they are most in demand, forms of public transportation make for chance encounters with others whom we may otherwise never encounter. Cities, especially the larger ones, are rich in human variety, and one way to ensure that we limit our contact with this enriching variety is to sequester us in our individual self-driven vehicles. Sequestration of a sort is already far advanced. Most of us walk around mesmerized by screens and tuned in to our own individual soundscapes delivered via headphone. At home, this pattern of behavior continues, sadly. We are losing the ability to be with each other, whether that means having rich and complex and spontaneous interactions or simply occupying the same space, something that is not as mindless and mundane and trivial as it might appear, as psychologists and neuroscientists are beginning to understand. Self-driven cars, by eliminating the need for public transportation will only deepen this social atomization.
          The advocates of autonomous vehicles claim that eliminating public transportation cuts infrastructure costs, and they might even say, it would make for greener environs. But it is not clear whether the rise in the number of autonomous vehicles will not require comparable infrastructure, as the majority must drive such vehicles if they are to be a feasible option in the first place.
         And speaking of majorities, we are brought face to face with another problem: cost. It will surely be a while before self-driven cars are within the means of the majority. And assuming that they could become a viable proposition before the majority of the population owns them, they would in the interim be status symbols, affordable only by the wealthy.  Our already deeply divided society can do without such obvious markers of status differentials.
	And then, there is the question of safety. We all know that computer algorithms are not infallible, that programs can be infected with viruses, that hardware can malfunction, that computers are at the mercy of hackers. A human driver has a repertoire of skills to call upon in the event of unforeseen situations: an accident, bad weather, a terrorist attack. As far as I am aware, computer algorithms that function in very dynamic situations without human intervention are not that plentiful. What then would a rider do, especially if they are not tech savvy, in a complex and fluid situation? 
	You may argue that we are daily rendered helpless by such things as drunk or inattentive motorists, with dire consequences that are clear from the statistics about accidents. I cannot disagree. And yet, I find I must stand my ground. 
	I ask you to imagine a situation where your self-driving car cannot adapt in time, something as simple as a failure of traffic signals or a sudden thunderstorm, never mind a hacked program or a spectacular terrorist attack. Nor can the cars of anyone else. And you're all headed for disaster with no recourse. Rendered utterly passive where once you might have attempted some solution, no matter its efficacy. 
	It is this removal of human agency, individual and collective,  that I worry about. This helplessness that is not natural human vulnerability but rather thralldom to machines. Unlike human vulnerability, which can breed virtues like compassion and pity, this thralldom is not generative, it is lifeless in the way of an inorganic thing. 
	I do not wish to be thus reduced.

draft1:
Today's society is seeing the start of a new era in technology, where artificial intelligence is becoming mainstream, and so is our reliance on them. This includes the gradual integration of self-driving cars, or cars that rely on computer programming to give it directions.

This can be a great improvement to driving safety, as this is potentially a much secure form of driving. Humans are easily fallible -- they can get distracted, feel sleepy, become under the influence -- and drastically increase their chance of accidents. Computers, however, do not succumb to such mistakes.

Additionally, they are able to communicate with other computers easily, so if there were multiple self-driving cars on the road, they can all sense each other within seconds and know how to navigate from there.

However, computers are not 100% fool-proof. They are hackable. They can be fooled. And they rely on more people to "opt-in" and drive their own self-driving car, otherwise, accidents will still happen.

But the thing is, dear reader, that all these cons can be remedied with time. We are currently seeing the beginning of this new stage in automation, but the idea of relying on new technology to propel us forward in society is a long-standing tradition. This is a natural process.

The more we work on this technology, the safer it will become. We will develop new software updates to prevent its hackability. We will improve upon its spatial awareness and its adaptability in order for the programs to be fooled as easily, or at all. And the more we improve upon it, the more widespread the technology will be, which will cause its pricepoint to decrease and in turn make self-driving cars much more accessible. 

There is also the concern about the start of a dystopian-like society. "What if we become too reliant on our technology and then it decides to stop working, or it turns against us?" To that, dear reader, I say: science-fiction is still science-fiction. Just because we learned how to use a calculator does not mean we have stopped learning mathematics in school. Just because we have the autopilot in planes does not mean we do not stop teaching pilots how to fly. And just because we can use self-driving cars does not mean we will stop learning how to drive.

But, dear reader, I will definitely tell you this: stick shift deserves to become forgotten and buried forever.

In today's society, we are seeing the start of a new era in technology where artificial intelligence (AI) is becoming mainstream, and so is our reliance on them. Examples of this can be found in our homes, social media, everyday retail, and other facets of our lives. 
	Many dystopian books and films would like you to believe that the steady integration of AI is the start of our societal downfall, but this is simply not the case--take self-driving cars for example. For those of you who are unfamiliar, self-driving cars are vehicles that rely on AI to give itself directions and to literally drive itself without any human interaction. 
	In general, this is a great improvement to driving safety as it can provide a much secure form of driving. Humans are easily fallible--they get distracted, they get sleepy, and they can choose to drive while under the influence--which can drastically increases the chance of accidents. Computers, however, are literally unable to succumb to such mistakes and are therefore safer. 
	Additionally, computers are able to communicate with other computers easily, unlike humans who have no form of communication with any of the other drivers in close proximity. This advantage allows computers in self-driving cars to sense each other within seconds and negotiate the road together. 
	However, many people think that if we were to rely on these AI-driven cars, it would become a gradual demise for us. Self-driving cars are hackable. They can be fooled. And they rely on people en masse to "opt-in" and buy their own self-driving cars, otherwise a single self-driving car cannot communicate with a vehicle which cannot "talk back."
	But what these naysayers are forgetting is that all of these downfalls can be remedied with time--we are only seeing the beginning of this new stage in automation. It is a natural process to introduce new technology into society and wait for newer models to come out until it is perfected. By dismissing the new technology in its early stages without giving it the chance to improve with time would be like telling a child to stop practicing at something because they were awful in the beginning. 
	Here is a simple example of how we can slowly rely on technology to assist us: traffic lights. Way before we relied on traffic lights to help guide us through intersections, we had traffic guards to help us. But over time, we allowed ourselves to accept traffic lights, instead of a human, to help us through. And of course, the traffic light wasn't perfect or even similar to the version we have today! It also had a simpler code and fewer systems to help us, but we let it stay up and now we have near-perfect traffic light controls and coordination systems. 
	The point is, the more we work on this technology, the safer it will become, but only if we give it a chance to grow and perfect itself. This is not to say that the negative points aren't valid or accurate observations on the current self-driving cars we have today. But we tend to forget that this is not an indication of what it could be. Of what it could provide for us. And how helpful and much safer it could actually be for us in the long-term. 
        Manufacturers, scientists, and computer scientists alike will develop new software updates to prevent its hackability. Will improve upon its spatial awareness and its adaptability in order for the programs to be fooled as easily. And with further improvement, the more common the technology will become and cause its pricepoint to decrease, which in turn will make self-driving cars much more accessible and allow a wider population to purchase their own.
	There is also the concern about the start of a dystopian-like society. "What if we become too reliant on our technology, and then it decides to just stop working or turn against us?" To that, I say: science-fiction is still science-fiction. Just because we learned how to use a calculator does not mean we have stopped learning mathematics in school. Just because we have the autopilot feature in planes does not mean we do not stop teaching pilots how to fly. And just because we give cars the knowledge of the road does not mean that all of a sudden it is going to drive off without us.
	But I will definitely tell you this: stick shift deserves to become forgotten and buried forever, and if you are one of the few who miss such a time, then maybe self-driving cars were never meant for you to begin with.
